responseSet.title: A Note on Freud's Scientific Imagination
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

A Note on Freud's Scientific Imagination

Leo Spiegel

Leo Spiegel

Leo Spiegel  

One of the striking, and as yet uncomprehended aspects of genius, is its ability to generate the necessary mental voltage to throw an illuminating spark across poles of knowledge that are apparently unrelated. This rare quality, distinguishing genius from talent, is seen in several of Freud's contributions, but nowhere more strikingly than in his schema of the basic functioning of the psychic apparatus, as developed in the seventh chapter of the Interpretation of Dreams. Convinced by numerous analyses that dreams, like hysterical symptoms, were meaningful and amenable to scientific interpretation, and persuaded by general concepts of evolution that they represented a primitive form of mental activity, Freud developed an hypothesis that enabled him to pass from the content of the dream and the rules for its interpretation, to the form and function of the total 'machinery' of the mind.

Approximately a half century later, the distinguished neurophysiologist Adrian, in the first Ernest Jones lecture, 1 proposed a theory of brain functioning which is substantially identical with Freud's hypothesis of the functioning of the psychic apparatus, as published in 1900. My brief note proposes to demonstrate this similarity, since it is both of biographical and psycho-analytic interest.

Essentially both hypotheses aim to provide answers to the same questions, but Adrian's formulation of these questions is somewhat more explicit. 'The problem is to explain how fresh activity arises, how it is directed to a particular purpose … and why it ceases when the aim is accomplished.' Freud is interested in answering another question: why the instinctual energy offered by the unconscious during sleep is only in the service of wish fulfilment. But in the course of answering this question, Freud actually follows the same lines stated in more detail by Adrian.

Freud utilizes his basic assumptions: the tendency of the primitive psychic apparatus to keep itself as free of excitation as possible; the aim of an instinctual drive to re-cathect perceptions previously associated with its gratification; the two well-known systems, one governed by the pleasure principle, the other by the reality principle; and the reality principle preventing the ego regression which would bring about hallucinatory wish-fulfilment.

responseSet.title: On the Mechanisms of Spacing and Crowding Emotions
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

On the Mechanisms of Spacing and Crowding Emotions11

Otto Sperling M.D.

Otto Sperling

Otto E. Sperling, M.D.
BROOKLYN, N.Y.

Otto Sperling  

Defences against emotions are more basic and archaic and were studied earlier than defences against instinctual drives. As early as 1900, in the Interpretation of Dreams, Freud mentioned several defences against affects, namely, repression, postponement, and displacement. Later he described isolation, change of quality, projection and introjection. There are special defences against feelings of guilt, for instance, sharing and borrowing of guilt, and regression. Against anxiety there are additional defences, e.g. denial, active anticipation, in the turning from passivity to activity, in libidinization, etc. Asceticism and intellectualization, which Anna Freud describes as defences against the sexual upsurge in adolescence2 are at the same time defences against the sexual affect.

In this paper I am trying to describe a mechanism which I have been able to observe in a number of cases whereby, out of fear of an excess of emotion, the affect is subdivided and the parts experienced at different times. In the psychological sphere this is similar to certain surgical operations which are performed in two parts, with an interval during which the patient may recover. In trying to explain this mechanism I am faced with the difficulty which complicates every psycho-analytic study, that of overdetermination. It is usually the mechanism of isolation which is also involved. I would therefore ask you to believe me that I have gone through the usual psycho-analytic interpretations of their peculiarities with these patients.

Jean, who suffered at that time from various phobias, was engaged and had to arrange the date for her wedding. She would have liked to elope, but her parents insisted on a formal wedding. For her a formal wedding meant a theatrical performance which she contemplated with some stage fright; but the actual wedding meant more to her—namely, arriving at a decision after a long period of doubt as to whether she was making the right choice of a husband. Besides this there was a fear of defloration. About three weeks before the wedding she persuaded her fiancé to get married in court, clandestinely. Thus the affect connected with making the decision and with defloration was separated by three weeks from the affect connected with the official wedding. She arranged for this subdivision out of fear that her emotion would be unbearable if there were no interval. As can be easily guessed from this behaviour, it was not possible for her to reach a vaginal orgasm. In her sexual relations with her husband, achieving an orgasm at the clitoris had to be separated from giving an orgasm to him.

'On the Psychopathology of Orgasm.'

Sylvan Keiser. Psychoanalytic Quarterly, 1947, Vol. XVI, No. 3, p. 378.

The detailed analysis of subjective feelings and fantasies during the sexual act indicates that the anxieties centre about passivity in both male and female patients. Men equate the penis and women a fantasied penis with the maternal nipple and both fear the hostility that was activated by their oral conflicts. Men regard the penis with which they have endowed women as a nipple and their own penis as an oral receptive organ.

Sidney Tarachow

Sidney Tarachow

Sidney Tarachow  

Two cases illustrating psychosomatic phenomena provoked by dreams were studied. Various conflictful situations led to powerful erotic and aggressive forces which in one case found expression through the vegetative system as oedema and in the other case through the cortex in epileptic convulsive seizures. The psychic energy released by the conflict situation initiates an altered physiological state. The symptoms in both cases were precipitated by dreams involved with characteristic conflict situations. Attacks could be re-created or newly provoked or avoided by means of hypnosis.

Sidney Tarachow

Sidney Tarachow

Sidney Tarachow  

Sixty-six cases of parkinsonism, mostly senile degenerative or postencephalitic, were studied in non-psycho-analytic interviews. Some of the patients also had Rorschach and handwriting studies. The author concludes that parkinsonism is characteristic of a certain personality type. This type is characterized by an urge toward motor activity and aggression in conflict with rigid moralistic tendencies. Various constitutional and psychological causes are enumerated. The disease appears when personality attitudes are interfered with.

Totem Feast in Modern Dress

Sidney Tarachow. The American Imago, 1948, Vol. 5, No. 1, pp. 65–69.

The author considers the modern banquet with its speaker and entertainment a derivative of the totem feast. In the original custom the symbolism of eating the totem animal indicated directly the aggression. In the modern custom the forces are displaced differently. The eating now carries little of the original meaning. The token of submission to an authority is carried out by listening to a speaker. The rituals connected with this indicate the ambivalence of both the speaker and the audience. These rituals include telling jokes and the toastmaster's introduction: this introduction anticipates the hostilities of the audience and also leads the audience to idealize the speaker. The speaker controls the audience, yet he shows signs of fear. The audience hates the speaker, yet is eager to be led to idealize him. The destruction of the father figure takes place in the entertainment, when the younger members of the group are given an opportunity to act out the parts of the authorities and to destroy the authorities. The function of the feast is fulfilled. The father and sons are reconciled. The father controls his sons and overcomes his fear of them. The sons accept his control, but also destroy him, identify with him and succeed in remaining friends with him.

Author's Abstract.

63

'The Bear in the Haunted Mill.'

Geza Roheim. The American Imago, 1948, Vol. 5, No. 1, pp. 70–82.

Sidney Tarachow

Sidney Tarachow

Sidney Tarachow  

The author discusses various European (mostly Hungarian) folk tales which are attempts to solve castration fear provoked by Oedipal rivalries and by the sight of the female genitals. Because of the well-known sexual symbolism of the mill, grinding mills are haunted by bears or other dangerous figures. Generally the hero vanquishes a bear or other dangerous creature by clamping a vice on to some extremity (hand, nose) of the creature and later by threatening the villain with the vagina of the woman. The sight of the vagina is sufficient to send the haunting animals fleeing in terror.

The hero who overcomes the bear frequently gets the miller's daughter as a reward. Often the bear or demon wants to learn to play the hero's violin (playing the violin equals masturbation). These fairy tales are similar to certain nightmares in which fear of the vagina (fear of castration) is projected on to a bear. In such a solution the hero gets the girl but the bear flees in panic. The Oedipal rivalry for the mother is sometimes manifest and father and son (demon and hero) try to castrate one another. One of the ways in which the hero overcomes his fear of masturbation with its unconscious incestuous meaning is to teach the demon to play the violin. Here again, masturbation anxiety is relieved by projection on to the father figure.

63

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Relation of Parental Authority to Children's Behaviour Attitudes

By Marian J. Radke. (Minnesota University Press. London: Geoffrey Cumberlege. Price 11 s. 6 d.)

Ruth Thomas

Ruth Thomas

Ruth Thomas  

This is an enquiry on the one hand into the attitudes adopted by a group of parents of good social and professional standing, in the upbringing of their children of nursery school age (3 years 10 months–5 years 10 months); and, on the other, into the social behaviour and attitudes to parental discipline of the children themselves; the objective being to determine the degree and significance of the interrelatedness of the two. The author addressed parallel questionnaires to both groups covering the severity of restrictions imposed in the home, the relative responsibility of both parents for control, the child's relations with his siblings and the parents' underlying philosophy of education. The material was sifted to yield information on the correspondence between parental and infantile attitudes as well as between these attitudes and the children's social behaviour as observed in standard situations involving projective techniques and in nursery school.

The assumption underlying the methodology of the enquiry, viz. that the subjects' reports upon their own attitudes can be taken at their face value, implies a disconcerting unawareness of the factor of ambivalence, and the unconscious defensive distortions to which the replies are open. The slight and inconclusive nature of the findings on the main issue may be attributable in part to this fact. In greater part they are due to the rather too simple assumption underlying the details of the questionnaires that the core of the parent child relationship at this age is to be found in the democratic or autocratic discipline the parent exerts in certain very generally defined social circumstances within the home. From an analytic viewpoint it is conceivable that an autocratic attitude may be the mark of a rejecting parent, and it is therefore possible to interpret in this light the loose connection which the enquiry establishes between this attitude on the parents' part, and over-submissive or otherwise unsocial behaviour from the children. The terms of the questionnaires do not however rule out the possibility that the group of democratic parents may have included those whose discipline was over-indulgent, so that the issue between democratic and autocratic is by no means clear cut.

[[RunningHead]]

Basic Mental Concepts, their Clinical and Theoretical Value

By Edward Glover, M.D. (London: Imago. Pp. 32. Price 3 s. 6 d.)

This outline of some of the basic psycho-analytic concepts was first presented at a Conference of European Psycho-Analysts held in Amsterdam in May, 1947. It is a reprint from the Psycho-Analytic Quarterly, New York, Vol. XVI, No. 4, 1947.

The author sets out to re-examine the basic mental concepts of Psycho-Analysis. No attempt is made here to render a comprehensive summary of its contents. Only a simplified outline will be given, and an exposition of a few more outstanding, and perhaps also more challenging, conclusions by way of orientation.

The author's main argument is, that since clinical analysis is not possible before the infant can apprehend the meaning of spoken interpretations, statements as to mental development during at least the first two years of life must of need be based on reconstructions. These reconstructions are bound to be dependent on preconceived theories. No hypothetical construction should be regarded as legitimate which does not correspond to the basic concepts on which psycho-analytical theory is founded. These basic concepts can thus serve as a test of new theories. The author does not think that any new analytic technique can ever solve these problems, nor, in his view, can behaviouristic studies. In this connection he uses the curious argument that such observations, to be of any service, must first be interpreted (my italics). 'Data of observation can only be of service provided they are expressed in metapsychological terms.' (Page 31.) In his view, although the complexity of early psychological organization can well be underestimated, the greater danger lies in overestimating it. He decries in this connection the use of concepts like 'primitive ego', 'Super ego', 'central position', or the assumption of highly developed mental mechanisms or complex phantasy formations at a very early age, as from the third to the sixth month of life, in favour of simpler concepts, such as fluctuations of instinctual charges and variations of affect. Development proceeds from the simpler to the more complex and so should our concepts.

[[RunningHead]]

Psychotherapy. Its Uses and Limitations

By D. R. Allison and R. G. Gordon. (Geoffrey Cumberledge: Oxford University Press. Pp. 160. Price 8 s. 6 d.)

H. Thorner

H. Thorner

H. Thorner  

This book is written for the general physician to help him 'to differentiate between those patients who present mental symptoms and who can really benefit by psychotherapy, and those who present similar symptoms or diseases which must be recognized as not amenable to this form of treatment, though they may be treated by other methods.' Chapters are devoted to several conditions: psychoneuroses, psychosomatic conditions, temperamental instabilities, visceral neuroses, endocrine conditions, psychoses, mental deficiencies, psychopathic personalities, etc.

The various types of psychotherapy are enumerated in the introductory chapter which deals also with the development of psychotherapy. It contains a short account of the development of medical psychology from Charcot to Freud, Jung and Adler with some paragraphs on the various attempts which were made to shorten treatment. Some space is devoted to the attitude of the general public and the medical profession to psycho-analysis. The public's sceptical and antagonistic attitude is attributed to the exaggerated claims of psycho-analysts, and in the authors' view this attitude changed, post or propter hoc, after the report by the 1929 British Medical Association Committee on psycho-analysis. In this, and in many other remarks, the authors show their attitude to unconscious processes. For instance, in the chapter on psychoneuroses, where the significance of the transference situation is mentioned, they speak about the Oedipus complex which they 'not uncommonly' find in their patients, but usually in a modified and attenuated form. They reject the Oedipus situation as of general occurrence, considering another situation to be much more fundamental and important, namely that of loss of maternal love and protection. The crucial point of the psychotherapy they advise is formulated in these words: 'It is the duty of the psychotherapist to bring the patient to see the loss has been more imagined than real. …' The approach appears to be mainly one emphasizing the conscious. This attitude is reflected also in remarks about the separation of responsibility for psychoanalytical and physical treatments for which they do not seem to see much need.

[[RunningHead]]

Normal and Abnormal Psychology

A Précis for Junior Students, Nurses, Occupational Therapists. Welfare Workers and others. By J. Ernest Nicole, (London: Allen & Unwin. Pp. 96. Price 8 s. 6 d.)

H. Thorner

H. Thorner

H. Thorner  

This little book is not meant to be a text-book, but it is written on text-book lines. The subject is systematically discussed under separate headings. Recognized theories are explained and discussed with arguments for and against. The first part of the book deals with 'Psychology' refreshingly leaving out a good deal of academic psychology which is useless for nurses for whom this précis is primarily written. This part deals with definitions of psychology, instincts, conditioned reflexes, etc., and with personality and character problems. The second part is headed 'Psychopathology'. It deals with the causation of mental disorders, the unconscious mind, psycho-analysis, the Jungian and Adlerian schools, psychosomatic medicine, and applied psychology. The book is written in a lucid style, and will be easily understood. The author has extracted freely from his earlier book 'Psychopathology'.

For the psycho-analyst the treatment of psycho-analysis is of particular interest. The author divides the teachings of psycho-analysis into two parts, one that is widely accepted (the unconscious, mechanisms of defence, analysis of dreams, etc.), and another one that contains controversial material (sexual theory). The widely accepted part of psycho-analysis is contained in a chapter entitled 'Repression and Symptom Formation', but the reader learns of its psycho-analytical origin only from one sentence at the end of the following chapter which is entitled 'Psycho-Analysis (Freud)' and contains a short account of infantile sexuality, fixation, regression, the Oedipus conflict, etc.

The value of a book of this nature depends on its ability to give the psychologically uneducated reader a fair impression of the subject. This the author has achieved, but its lucidity is gained by a very schematic treatment of the subject—of which the divisions of normal and abnormal psychology are examples. It is not quite easy to understand why instincts should be dealt with under the heading of normal psychology while the development of the sexual instinct is dealt with under psychopathology.

responseSet.title: 'The Cornerstone Ceremony.': Jacques Schnier.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

'The Cornerstone Ceremony.'

Jacques Schnier.

Frank Berner

Frank Berner

Frank Berner  

Psychoanalytic Review, 1947, Vol. XXXIV, No. 3, pp. 357–369.

The custom of consecrating buildings and foundations dates back to the early Egyptian dynasties. The attention given to the cornerstone in the early stages of construction appears to stem from its position as a foundation, a beginning or a boundary stone. The history of the dedication rites shows that relics, souvenirs, documents, and live human beings have been buried in the foundation. All these represent a sacrificial victim, a symbol of the dead father. In connection with the beginning of a building a situation is set up in the participant's unconscious mind which causes him to sense danger. Anxiety is set up which must be dissipated by murdering the father. The undertaking of building a new structure is such as to arouse the wrath of the father. Therefore, he must be done away with so as not to interfere. It is as if the undertaking is against the father's wishes and cannot be successful if he is present and alive. Symbolically, the new structure is the mother or the mother's womb. By means of the portals, gates or doors, she will become available to him for symbolic intercourse or symbolic return to her for protection. But the thought of recovering the mother arouses terrific fear of the father. It causes an anxiety of such potential that it can only be released by eliminating the father. This is done by destroying and burying him at the very entrance to the womb. Henceforth each time a superstitious man enters or leaves a house, a church, or a city, he is unconsciously reminded that buried beneath the threshold lies the dreaded father and henceforth he need never be feared again.

178

responseSet.title: 'Research in Psychotherapy.': Thomas M. French.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

'Research in Psychotherapy.'

Thomas M. French.

Martin Grotjahn

Martin Grotjahn

Martin Grotjahn  

Amer. Journ. Psychiatry, 1948, Vol. 105, No. 3, p. 229.

A therapist's first obligation is to his patient. While treatment is still in progress the therapist cannot free himself from the emotional pressure of his therapeutic responsibilities; even after the therapeutic aim has been reached and his obligations as a physician have been discharged, he more or less continuously focuses his interest on evaluating his efforts as a therapist, and, therefore, always has difficulties to evaluate properly the therapeutic techniques he has employed.

Research in psychotherapy should not be confused with evaluation of therapeutic results. There is a true reason why it is practically impossible to learn from the usual statistical methods. Comparing the patient's behaviour symptoms before and after treatment is far different from trying to describe what actually happened during psychotherapy.

180

[[RunningHead]]

'The Significance of the Home for the Child's Emotional Development During the First Six Years.'

Kate Friedlander.

Jour. Ment. Sci., 1948, Vol. XCIV, No. 395, p. 305.

Basing her deductions on psycho-analytical theory the author tries to explain the significance of the family setting for the child's emotional development during the first six years of life.

Three aspects of development are chosen—the modification of primitive instinctive drives, the first object relationship and super-ego formation to exemplify the thesis. Attention is drawn to the fact that although the family setting is far superior to any other medium for the growing child, much work will have to be done with future parents to allow a satisfactory outcome.

Author's Abstract.

responseSet.title: 'Graduate Residency Training in Psychoanalytic Medicine.': Sandor Rado.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

'Graduate Residency Training in Psychoanalytic Medicine.'

Sandor Rado.

Martin Grotjahn

Martin Grotjahn

Martin Grotjahn  

New York, American Journal of Psychiatry, Vol. CV, No. 2, August, 1948, pp. 111–115.

Rado gives a short history of training in psychoanalysis from the early days in Vienna to the present situation in New York. The first systematic course of training in psycho-analysis was organized in the early 'twenties at the Berlin Psychoanalytic Institute. At that time the curriculum was divided into three parts: didactic analysis, theoretical instruction, and practical work. The first psycho-analytic institute in the United States was organized according to the model of the Berlin Institute.

The analytic training institute saved the art and science of psycho-analysis but they could offer no full compensation for the exclusion of psychoanalysis from the medical schools in the United States. More and more this exclusion proved harmful to both sides. The establishment of the Psychoanalytic Clinic for Training and Research at Columbia University, mostly due to Rado himself, has changed this situation. The Columbia plan of graduate residency training in psychoanalytic medicine, based on the facilities of the university, and now in operation for three academic years, requires three years of full-time graduate work. Under the Columbia system the students receive psycho-analytic clinical instruction from the beginning parallel with the theoretical programme. The purpose of this practical orientation is to introduce the student to the art of psycho-analytic observation and examination and acquaint him with the clinical variety of cases by unfolding before him in a comparative manner the gross psychodynamics of every case.

181

'Metamorphosis.'

Geza Roheim. The American Imago, November, 1948, Vol. V, No. 3, pp. 167–172.

Sidney Tarachow

Sidney Tarachow

Sidney Tarachow  

The author quotes myth and fairy-tale dealing with metamorphosis. Shape shifting belongs to the sphere of magic and the gods. Most of the examples deal with transformations of humans into animals and vice versa. Metamorphosis often deals with sexual phenomena and symbols, reproduction by splitting in two, impregnation, and resurrection after death. The author states that metamorphosis reflects the most essential attributes of the primary process, namely the motility of all cathexis. The resemblance of myth to dream indicates the dream origin of many myths. Symbol usage in metamorphosis and myth is considered another instance of the primary process.

[[RunningHead]]

'Kilroy was Here.'

Richard Sterba. The American Imago, November, 1948, Vol. V, No. 3, pp. 173–181.

Sidney Tarachow

Sidney Tarachow

Sidney Tarachow  

Sterba examines a legend which appeared among the American G.I.'s during the last World War. The words 'Kilroy was here' would be found in all sorts of public places, but also in the most inaccessible and dangerous places, and especially in latrines. It would be found under scrawled cartoons of a pregnant woman. The author notes that cutting the name in two gives kill-roi, the one who kills the king. Thus the legend is a revival of one of the most ancient legends. The emotions are so powerful this legend swept over the entire world. The enemy country is the mother. The Kilroy inscription has been found in the most inaccessible places. This inaccessibility gives it mother significance. It is a grandiose fulfilment of Oedipal wishes. Kilroy is the hero of all legends, the father murderer.

250

Psicoanálisis De La Melancolía

Edited by Ángel Garma and Luis Rascovsky. (Published by Asociacíon Psicoanalítica Argentina and 'El Ateneo', Buenos Aires, 1948. Pp. 519.)

Hedwig Hoffer

Hedwig Hoffer

Hedwig Hoffer  

This volume contains papers by Sigmund Freud, Karl Abraham, Richard Sterba, Sandor Rado, Helene Deutsch, Georg Gero, E. Pichon-Rivière, Ángel Garma, Edith Jacobson, Sandor Lorand, Betram D. Lewin, Melanie Klein, and has been compiled and introduced by Ángel Garma and Luis Rascovsky. The editors have set themselves the ambitious task to inform and to interest the medical reader and the psychologist about the psycho-analytic theory and practice of the manicdepressive states. E. Pichon-Rivière's History of manic-depressive States forms the first part of the book and introduces its reader to the findings of the non-analytical schools. The main part deals with the psycho-analytic investigations of melancholia. The development of thought on this subject is thrown into relief by extensive quotations from Freud's Obras Completas which also form the frame. The contributions of the other before-mentioned authors follow in chronological order. A summary by Ángel Garma concludes part 2. The third part is devoted to mania. A quotation from Freud's Wit and its relation to the Unconscious is followed by B. D. Lewin's 'Analysis and Structure of a transient hypomania' and Helene Deutsch's 'Chronische Hypomanie', all these serve as illustrations for the third part. Melanie Klein's contribution to the psychogenesis of manic depressive States forms the fourth and last part of the volume.

Patología Psicosomática

Compliación y prólogo del Doctor Arnaldo Rascovsky con la participación de los Doctores Cárcamo, Hardoy, Vega, Garma, Krapf, Langer, Pichon-Rivière, Rascovsky, Salerno, Sclossberg, Tallaferro. (Published by the Association Psicoanalitica Argentina and El Ateneo, Buenos Aires, 1948. Pp. 768.)

Hedwig Hoffer

Hedwig Hoffer

Hedwig Hoffer  

Twelve analysts have collaborated with Arnoldo Rascovsky to write this comprehensive text-book on Psychosomatic Pathology. The authors aim at a close collaboration between psycho-analysts and general physicians in order to reach a new understanding of pathological processes. They stress the importance of the psychological aspect in somatic illness and reaffirm their points by their own investigations. Their deliberations are based partially on the findings made in the course of analytic treatment and partially on psycho-analytically directed investigations of cases with similar symptoms and similar mechanisms.

Didactical considerations shape the layout of the material. Therefore an introductory chapter on the sexual development of the child in relation to psychosomatic problems precedes the clinical papers. Investigations into single organs or organ systems follow. The digestive tract, the respiratory system, blood circulation, neuro-psychiatry, gynæcology, endocrinology, obstetrics and traumatology are the topics under review. Throughout the authors make full use of the literature on psychosomatics and in a last chapter they give a summary of the more recent literature.

An extensive investigation into bronchial asthma and arterial hypertension are planned for the future.

258

responseSet.title: Heredity and Psycho-Analysis
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Heredity and Psycho-Analysis11

H. Van Der Waals

H. Van Der Waals

H. Van Der Waals  

It is an old problem which I shall discuss with you this evening and throughout the ages mankind has discerned its importance. Shakespeare indicates it in his own unrivalled manner where he makes Prospero judge Caliban:

A devil, a born devil, on whose nature Nurture can never stick; on whom my pains, Humanely taken, are all lost, quite lost; And as with age his body, uglier grows, So his mind cankers. The Tempest it, IV, i, 188–92.

Here nature denotes hereditary endowment and nurture the influence exercised by environment, and, as regards the effects produced on such endowment by the surroundings, Prospero shows a pessimistic view concerning Caliban. It is a point of view which has also prevailed in science and which still dominates the minds of many investigators.

It is not difficult to show that an hereditary endowment may actually exercise a fatal influence on the course of the bearer's existence. One example has to suffice. Is it not fate if a man, either in his younger years or at a riper age, begins to show the symptoms of Huntington's disease, deteriorating in a way that cannot be checked? This sufferer's destiny is a grievous one. More and more the normal control of the motility by the ego is replaced by almost entirely contrary conditions: the disordered motility breaks loose, the ego becomes subjected to it, and no longer rules. In the case of mental diseases springing from some inherited factor, the conditions appear to be far more complicated. Although in most cases of the endogenous psychoses the co-responsibility of other factors for the outbreak of the psychosis cannot be denied, cases do occur in which the endowment seems to possess such penetrating power that it scarcely needs auxiliary causes and could justifiably be considered fate.

It is not only these marked psychotic conditions whose causality must be sought in hereditary endowment. As early as 1876, Sir Francis Galton felt justified in asking whether nurture could 'do anything at all beyond giving instruction and professional training'. With regard to the formation of character, he expresses the opinion that there is no escape from the conclusion that nature prevails enormously over nurture (1). In this connection Galton made use of the image of the cuckoo, singing the same way in every part of the world though laying its eggs in the most diverse nests. In spite of analytical developmental psychology which has evolved since his time, Galton's point of view is still modern. Erbbiologische investigations of character of the last years, especially German ones, share Galton's point of view almost entirely. This view is to the effect that disturbances in adaptation, irrespective of organic injuries by infections, poisoning, etc., are always due to a less favourable endowment. Endowment becomes inevitable fate. Titles such as that of Pfahler's characterology Heredity as Fate and of J. Lange's interesting study Delinquency as Fate would then, despite their sensational admixture, be essentially quite justified. The weight of these authors' arguments for their opinions has to be tested; what has been said, however, justifies a serious consideration of the proportion that endowment signifies fate.

responseSet.title: Psychiatric and Social Processes
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Psychiatric and Social Processes

Leonard Sillman M.D.

Leonard Sillman

Leonard R. Sillman, M.D.
NEW YORK

Leonard Sillman  

The nature of the forces which cause historical change has ever been one of the most crucial and challenging problems facing the intelligence of man. There are two main obstacles to the achievement of a real comprehension of the dynamics of history. The first is the intense subjectivism of social thinking which far transcends even that seen in connection with psychological and religious phenomena. For every man's welfare, his hopes for security or fame, his freedom or possible enslavement, his life or death are involved in the apparently fickle winds that propel human events. Because of this subjectivism, even the most objective and detached of men tend to confuse fantasy with reality when under the pressure of the powerful wishes and fears roused by the course of world shaking political events.

The second reason for this costly gap in man's knowledge has been the fact that moral, political and economic theories have been formulated with scant knowledge of the nature of the prime mover in history—the human mind. The observers and thinkers of the past have been like men who would discover and treat disease with no understanding of human anatomy, physiology, or pathology. This obstacle was inevitable until the Promethian genius of Freud arose to found an objective science of the mind. For the first time in history there is now available information as to some of the factors causing the varieties of attitudes, preferences, and desires which are the basis for social change. The discoveries of Freud await adaptation to social problems to create a real science of historical change.

In order to be scientific about social phenomena it is necessary to maintain a strictly objective and biological view of the nature of man. Man must be recognized first as a mammal, with powerful sensual cravings, and secondly as a predatory, carniverous primate, the only higher member of the primate order to give up the defensive advantages of arboreal existence and become transformed into a predominantly aggressive, flesh-eating animal. These biological facts have left mankind with powerful sensual and aggressive drives which impel the individual to all extremes of behaviour. The restraint of these urges is derived primarily from man's sense of guilt and secondly from his sense of reality. According to all archæological evidence, these are very recent developments. Like all new innovations they are not securely or powerfully fixed in his native endowment.

Telepathic dreams occurred in patients when the analyst wished to participate in such happenings. The manifest content of the dreams relating to the analyst all had unpleasant connections for the analyst and referred to incidents which the analyst wished to avoid. The wish to participate in telepathic happenings helped overcome anxiety which would ordinarily inhibit recognition of repressed content of the analyst's unconscious.

60

A detailed discussion and criticism of Mrs. Klein's theories is presented by the author. His major contention is that the theory of residues is acceptable but its exclusive application is not. He believes that there is a failure to distinguish between clinical appearance and genetic origin. The theory of regression he says is almost completely discarded by Mrs. Klein. He believes that Mrs. Klein 'retrojects' into the earliest phases of physical development much that belongs to a later stage.

60

Sylvan Keiser

Sylvan Keiser

Sylvan Keiser  

The paper is divided into several sections. Under the Selection of Candidates he places foremost for the future analyst that he must have access to and the will to face his own unconsciousness. To be excluded are psychopathic personalities which include anti-social types, individuals in whom perversions have displaced normal genitality, unintegrated characters who change from one position to another. Those with rigid characters are unsuited for psycho-analysis. Four or five hours may be given to the introduction to analysis. This time is devoted to explaining free association and to correct misconceptions. The problem of the transference must be discussed. The course of the analysis must be therapeutic. The analyst may consider his work at an end when the analysand has it firmly established that he must continue and correct and make more profound his own analyses.

60

The training of unanalysed psychiatrists by psycho-analysts is strongly recommended by the author for psychotherapy. He refers to the advantages of such training in clinical psychiatry and cites some outstanding examples who have made contributions. (The reviewer would like to comment that unfortunately many people who are exposed to such teaching very soon consider themselves psycho-analysts.)

'Vision, Headache and the Halo.'

Phyllis Greenacre. Psychoanalytic Quarterly, 1947, Vol. XVI, No. 2, p. 177.

Sylvan Keiser M.D.

Sylvan Keiser

Sylvan Keiser  

A child experiences a stunning psychic blow, usually an overwhelming visual experience which has the effect of dazing and bewildering it. This produces the sensation of colours, lights, 'seeing stars', and is accompanied by intense emotional states. An erotized aggression is aroused. Headaches develop in association to situations that connect with the original trauma. The child develops a halo which he may throw away or endow upon someone else. Such people over-evaluate their love objects. Two detailed case histories are given to illustrate the points made.

60

Clinical material is presented to demonstrate that the postural behaviour of every patient has definite motivation. Postural changes occur with changes in the analysis and new postures are determined by the material released through the analysis. Postural reactions may express either anxiety or its repression. Movements are repeated until the tension is adequately discharged. The meaning of all postures and involuntary motor reactions is over-determined.

60

Sylvan Keiser

Sylvan Keiser  

Social prejudice, war, depression, revolution or death are all considered erroneously to be pathological. There is an individual psychopathology which is put in the service of the social reaction. The emotional origin of social prejudice leads to the assumption of a common source. Anti-semitism, anti-catholicism and anti-negro prejudices are described. The hatred and aggression of man as a member of a herd is always available. There is a conflict in man that he may be separated from the herd or lost in the herd. This provides a basic anxiety which utilizes projection.

61

There are many suggestions outlined by the author for the management and treatment of patients. The wishes for freedom must arise from the patient and not be determined by the physician's conception of what the patient should desire. Therapists should avoid patients whom they fear. Closed wards must be considered therapeutic and not punitive. When a psychotic patient refuses to come to the doctor, then the therapist should go to the patient.

61

The author offers several drawings to demonstrate that pictorial economy results in an economy of psychic energy in the observer. He is saved from a more confusing or complex problem. The author illustrates the observation that some persons treat other individuals as projections of their own unconscious trends. Case histories are discussed to illustrate this point. 'For example, a man may hate a woman and fear her because to him she represents a projection of his own poorly repressed trends which are hated and feared. From another viewpoint, such a man would react in this

61

way to a woman because she stimulates the femininity which he hates and fears.'

62

Lawrence S. Kubie. Psychoanalytic Quarterly, 1947, Vol. XVI, No. 4, p. 507.

Sylvan Keiser M.D.

Sylvan Keiser

Sylvan Keiser  

The author states his thesis as follows: 'the easy assumption of quantitative variables as the only ultimate explanation of every variation in behaviour is one of the seductive fallacies to which all psychological theorizing is prone.' There is a human need to want to measure and to evaluate quantitatively. The many uncontrolled variables in the analytic situation make it impossible to accurately evaluate how much, how intense a particular psychic event is. A quantitative formulation does not relate itself to the interplay of many conflicting forces. The so-called economic formulations are considered the weakest element in current theories of psychological causation.

62

Practical Psychiatry and Mental Hygiene

By Samuel W. Hartwell. (New York and London: McGraw-Hill. Pp. xvi + 439. Price 22 s. 6 d.)

W. Clifford Scott

W. Clifford Scott

W. Clifford Scott  

After an introduction to Psychiatry via Child Guidance, the author became Professor of Psychiatry at the University of Buffalo. Since 1945 he has been Supervisor of the Department of Mental Hygiene of the State of Michigan. This text-book is both for general and psychiatric nurses and is one of, if not indeed, the best text-book in the field. The first quarter of the book introduces Psychology as it is related to Psychiatry. Freudian Metapsychology and Meyerian Psychobiology are discussed most extensively. The middle half of the book covers most of the whole range of psychiatric problems, and many excellently summarized case histories are included. The last and perhaps the best quarter deals with Mental Hygiene throughout childhood and adulthood.

The author has deliberately attempted to write simply. This he has done. In contrast to the simplicity of the text one is perhaps surprised with the technical nature of the references given at the end of each chapter and also at the end of the book. Many of the most useful, special but elementary texts are not mentioned.

A useful Glossary is appended.

65

[[RunningHead]]

Fatigue and Impairment in Man

By S. Howard Bartley and Eloise Chute. (New York and London: McGraw-Hill. Pp. ix + 429. Price 33 s.)

W. Clifford Scott

W. Clifford Scott

W. Clifford Scott  

Many will be indebted to the authors for their organization of the scattered and contradictory items in research already done in the field of Fatigue and Impairment. Their work at the Dartmouth Eye Institute, Dartmouth Medical School, led to their recognizing the need for a consistent and comprehensive view of fatigue. They have reviewed work in all relevant fields. Fatigue is regarded 'as an experiential patten arising in a conflict situation in which the general alignment of the individual may be described as an aversion. This particular pattern involves feelings of limpness and bodily discomfort which, besides being undesirable in themselves, are frequently taken as tokens of inadequacy for activity. The subjective constituents of this fatigue pattern are not to be taken as epiphenomena, or as symptoms of fatigue, but as fatigue itself.' Impairment is used in referring to specific tissue conditions.

They conclude that to know more about fatigue 'more must be known about the person as a whole'. To them fatigue is not an entity which can be measured in quantitative units, but is a phenomena which evolves chiefly 'from sets of emergent relationships'.

The historical review of allied concepts, the reviews of sleep and other periodicities, mental fatigue, personal factors in the work situation, conflict and frustration, chronic fatigue and related syndromes will be especially interesting to psychiatrists and psycho-analysts.

So much work is summarized that it is perhaps invidious to note that depression as a synonym for fatigue is not dealt with as adequately as psychiatrists and psycho-analysts would desire. Nevertheless this will be an invaluable book of reference to all psycho-analysts who are interested in the symptoms of fatigue.

69

Psychiatric Examination of the School Child

By Muriel Barton Hafl. (London: Edward Arnold. Pp. viii + 368. Price 15 s.)

W. Gillespie

W. Gillespie

W. Gillespie  

In her preface the author states that this is not intended to be a text-book of child psychiatry, but to portray general principles helpful in routine clinical examination and diagnosis. Designed primarily for the medical practitioner, it is meant also for others engaged in social medicine. It will be found, however, that in fact the book is remarkably comprehensive and covers in a very adequate way all the clinical conditions commonly met with in psychiatric practice with schoolchildren, as well as many rarer ones. From the point of view of clinical description the book deserves high praise, and the way in which illustrative cases have been followed up is most commendable and excites admiration and envy.

For the most part, the author's approach follows ordinary child guidance lines, though there is little to suggest that she makes much use of a psychiatric social worker in taking histories. The striking departure from customary usage is in her recommendation that the child should be seen first, before any history has been obtained, apart from the referral note. It is hard to believe that any advantage thus gained in initial rapport with the child is not heavily outweighed by the obvious disadvantage of not knowing what one is seeing the child about.

As this is not a psychiatric text-book, there is naturally little emphasis on ætiology; but it is surprising to find so little reference to the effect of early separation from the mother, apart from a brief warning of the danger of recommending removal of a young child from home.

There is, then, much to be said in favour of this book. It has, however, deficiencies which cannot but appear grave to psycho-analysts. The clinical pictures given are clear, but the children are seen exclusively from outside, that is, from the adult point of view, and there is no sign of any understanding of the child's deeper motivations. Indeed, this book betrays little evidence that Freud ever existed, and one is a little surprised to find in the chapter on treatment that the author is aware of the activities of child analysts. Dynamic factors are almost completely neglected. For readers of this Journal these are obviously very serious criticisms, and it is therefore worth reiterating that in other respects the book is a remarkably competent and reliable manual, full of valuable practical advice for those entering the field of child psychiatry, and up to date in its references to administrative matters, e.g. the 1944 Education Act. There is a bibliography of some 150 references.

'The Concurrent Analyses of Married Couples.'

Bela Mittelman.

Sylvan Keiser

Sylvan Keiser

Sylvan Keiser  

Psychoanalytic Quarterly, Vol. XVII, 1948, No. 2, p. 182.

The paper is a report on the simultaneous treatment of twelve married couples with a successful result in eleven. Frequently the analyst receives information from one spouse that is crucial in the treatment of the other partner. Transference reactions are described and they can be successfully managed. The greatest difficulty arises if one mate has a negative therapeutic reaction, or terminates treatment. Details of the management of treatment of several couples is given with suggestions for the handling of delicate situations.

The opinion of Freud and other authors is reviewed before presenting clinical data to substantiate the author's own findings. Through dreams the author demonstrates the unconscious olfactory stimulus that exists in the transvestite. The theme of the article is emphasized by the discussion of fetishism in the transvestite with the importance given to the smell of the object by such patients.

[[RunningHead]]

'The Effects of Shock Treatment on the Ego.'

John Frosch and David Inpastato.

Sylvan Keiser

Sylvan Keiser

Sylvan Keiser  

Psychoanalytic Quarterly, Vol. XVII, 1948, No. 2, p. 226.

An organic brain disease interferes with the adaptive process of the ego, leaving a patient more vulnerable to assault both from without and from within. The ego will be overwhelmed unless new defence mechanisms are erected. The above applies to the artificially induced organic state produced by shock treatment. The personal variations in reactions to the organic defect are described and demonstrate the individual psychological factor in the clinical syndrome. Several case histories are given. The authors conclude: (1) a practically complete dissolution of the ego. This leads to regression to an archaic narcissistic ego as a defence. The regression to a more primitive ego structure with its disruption of ego boundaries, or body image produces anxiety with a feeling of disintegration. Simultaneously a disintegration of ego defences occurs. This permits a welling up of previously repressed inner impulses. New repressions must take place or partial integration at a more primitive level of defence. In a successful case the ego realigns its defences on an even higher level of integration.

179

Myths from antiquity, incantations and customs from many lands are cited to prove the thesis—the thread of life is the umbilical cord and the final separation from the world is but a repetition of the first separation when the physical unity of mother and child is disrupted. responseSet.title: The Reach of the Mind: By J. B. Rhine, Professor of Psychology, Duke University, U.S.A. (London:  Faber and Faber Ltd., 1948.  Pp. 188.  Price 10 s. 6 d.)
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Reach of the Mind

By J. B. Rhine, Professor of Psychology, Duke University, U.S.A. (London: Faber and Faber Ltd., 1948. Pp. 188. Price 10 s. 6 d.)

Marjorie Brierley

Marjorie Brierley

Marjorie Brierley  

The experimental investigation of 'psi phenomena' has now become a recognized branch of psychological research and this is due, to no small extent, to the work done since 1930 by Professor Rhine and his colleagues at Duke University, North Carolina. Although much of it deals with tests carried out there, the book is not merely a departmental report but a survey of the origins and growth of para-psychology in which due credit is given to other American and foreign pioneers, e.g. in this country, Dr. Soal and the late Whately Carington. The author regards 'psi phenomena' as a challenge to materialist conceptions and as demanding a return from 'cerebrocentric' to 'psychocentric' views of man and his relation to the Universe.

Psycho-analysts are so accustomed to thinking in terms of mental processes that they may feel the author is flogging a dead horse in his first chapter 'The Central Question About Man'. It should be borne in mind, however, that Watsonian Behaviourism achieved a far greater vogue and more influence in America than in this country, where, in the main, theories of psycho-physical interaction or parallelism have tended to prevail over epiphenomenalism in academic circles. While advocating a psychocentric view of man, the author's own philosophical leanings are towards neutral monism.

Professor Rhine presents his review in the form of eight steps in the provision of evidence for psychocentrism. Chapter II describes the early work on telepathy which, by 1930, had made it appear credible. Chapter III registers the second step taken by the establishment of extra-sensory perception (ESP, clairvoyance) through experiments with special cards (ESP cards). It was now realized that much of the earlier work on telepathy did not discriminate between clairvoyance and telepathy. Further tests led, by 1940, to 'the working hypothesis that there is but one ESP, of which clairvoyance and telepathy are different manifestations.'

Chapter IV, 'The Reach of the Mind in Space' describes the third step, the procedures which attested the extra-physical or non-physical nature of ESP by showing that it is free from spatial dependence. Indeed, some subjects scored better at a distance from the experimenter and the test material than at close range.

Psycho-Analysis and the Social Sciences

Vol. I. Edited by Geza Roheim. (New York: International Universities Press. Pp. 427. Price $7.50.)

R. Money-Kyrle

R. Money-Kyrle

R. Money-Kyrle  

This is the first volume of a new Annual, which as its name implies is devoted to the application of psycho-analysis to the social sciences. It is subdivided into parts dealing respectively with Anthropology, Mythology, Religion, Literature, History and Sociology, and contains some fifteen articles by different contributors together with a general introduction by Dr. Roheim.

An Annual of this kind clearly fulfils a definite need, and, under the editorship of one who is already renowned for the versatility of his constructive imagination in the field of psycho-analytical anthropology, it should do much to promote the development of the new and important sciences with which it deals.

It certainly deserves and will receive the good wishes of all analysts.

68

responseSet.title: The 'Passing of the Oedipus Complex' in Greek Drama
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The 'Passing of the Oedipus Complex' in Greek Drama

Mark Kanzer

Mark Kanzer

Mark Kanzer
NEW YORK

Mark Kanzer  

I

The attention focussed by Freud on Sophocles' Oedipus Tyrannus has not been transferred, strangely enough, to the sequel, Oedipus at Colonus, which is integrally related to the problems of the earlier play. Whereas Oedipus Tyrannus confronts men with the disastrous results of their fundamental unconscious drives, Oedipus at Colonus depicts the process of restitution and social application of the same forces in a manner which anticipates and closely resembles Freud's own formulations as to the passing of the Oedipus complex through super-ego development.

The career of Oedipus did not end when he blinded himself after discovering that he had inadvertently carried out the prophesy that he would murder his father and marry his mother. He later went into exile from Thebes, the city he had ruled, and for twenty years (a generation!) wandered miserable and homeless through many lands, accompanied only by his daughter Antigone. Oedipus at Colonus deals with the refuge and peace which he finally found, and with his death.

At the beginning of the play, Oedipus and Antigone rest momentarily in a grove at Colonus, outside Athens. The natives warn them that they have committed sacrilege in entering the holy place of the Eumenides and are completely horrified when they discover that the stranger is the notorious Oedipus. The exiled king feels, however, that his accidental intrusion into the haunts of the Fates, or Eumenides, is a signal which he has awaited from the gods that his trials are over, and implores the alarmed populace to summon the Athenian king, Theseus. While awaiting Theseus, a second daughter of Oedipus, Ismene, arrives with the message that oracles have recently proclaimed that the grave of Oedipus will have great powers and that consequently rival disputants for the throne of Thebes will seek to gain possession of him. Oedipus rejoices that 'the gods who overthrew him now sustain him' but entertains only bitterness to the Thebans who drove him into exile. When Theseus arrives and shows himself well disposed, Oedipus offers him the promised power of his grave if the Athenian king will provide him with refuge and protection for the remainder of his days. Theseus agrees and the stage is set for two dramatic interviews with the contending rulers of Thebes.

responseSet.title: Recent German Literature on Psycho-Analysis
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Recent German Literature on Psycho-Analysis

W. Hoffer

W. Hoffer

W. Hoffer  

Reports from Central Europe evince the reawakening of psycho-analytic thought and activity in Germany and Austria and the resumption of publishing activities on a larger scale by Swiss psycho-analysts. From the publications under review, however, one cannot help but realize that the second world war, unlike the first, has deeply affected the individual psycho-analyst's mode of thinking, and, judged by the choice of subjects and the ways of presentation, has kept him in isolation even from his colleagues on the other side of the neighbouring frontier. Thus it can be no surprise, if psycho-analytic thinking in Central Europe went various ways and appears to be actuated by the different degrees of physical and psychological stress to which belligerent and neutrals were exposed. It should be kept in mind that the psycho-analytic periodicals in the German language had to be discontinued and that so far no provision has been made for the exchange of clinical and theoretical studies among the Central European psycho-analysts.

With one exception no new contribution has come to the reviewer's notice from Germany, which in the past added so much to psycho-analytic literature from the centres in Berlin and Frankfort. Almost the same is true of Vienna. Only Swiss psycho-analysts have fully resumed their publishing activities, which at present aim rather at spreading than at deepening psycho-analytic knowledge.

69

[[RunningHead]]

Co-Operation Tolerance and Prejudice

By Samuel Lowy, M.D. (Published by Routledge and Kegan Paul Ltd., 1948. Pp. 318. Price 21 s.)

W. Brown

W. Brown

W. Brown  

The author of this book is a psychotherapist with a wide experience of the analytic treatment of psychoneurotics. He is already well known for his deep interest in the fundamental problems of psychology and of social life. His main object is to help towards the creation of a new synthesis of psychological theory which shall take account of both the rational and the irrational elements contributing to the developing personality, and which shall be able to provide a practical guide to the improvement of human personality in all respects. He feels that psycho-analytic theory, despite its great power to provide insight and cure in individual cases, is a failure and even a folly in practice when applied to the social sphere. This he appears to attribute to the neglect of conscious factors and of the environmental situation as they affect the individual. For instance, there is 'The general spirit permeating the immediate and broader environment of the maturing youth, the actual significance accorded to the individual, the amount of appreciation or obstruction he meets, the real attitude towards dutifulness or selfishness immanent in the actual life of society and the legal administration, these and a great number of other phenomena are factors moulding the personality down to the deep subconscious spheres.'

Psycho-analysts may be annoyed by the way in which the author appears to put psycho-analysis in its place. But consideration will show that he is right, and that there is indeed a wide field between psycho-analysis and sociology awaiting detailed study. If we believe, as I think we must, that a constant metamorphosis is taking place between man and his environment, then it is entirely reasonable to suppose that if we learn enough about the process we shall also learn how to manipulate the factors in a scientific and constructive manner.

Dr. Lowy provides a broad and searching examination of the topics raised by the title of his book. It will be of interest and value to all serious students of social psychology, and to many others besides. After reading the book, I am encouraged to believe not only that a new psychological synthesis is required but also that it is on its way and promises practical results in the forseeable future.

The prepsychotic cannot integrate with his ego his superego with its excessive narcissistic libido. The author questions the validity of reconstructing events in the first two years of life from knowledge gained from adults or psychotics. He postulates a theory of nuclear ego development which allows an ample representation of early ego development arising from expression of the various components of early instinctual life. In this way ontogenetic ego and superego elements can be represented with more freedom than with a rigid concept of an oral ego. He does not believe the total ego function should be considered during the first two years of life, a period he prefers to call embryonic. Great emphasis is laid to the danger of over-evaluating fantastic reconstructions for the infant.

62

Sidney Tarachow

Sidney Tarachow  

Thirty-three cases of rheumatoid arthritis were studied, one half in therapeutic sessions: the cases were almost all women. The general psychodynamic background was a chronic, inhibited hostile aggressive state as a reaction to the earliest masochistic dependence on the mother that was carried over to the father and to all human relationships including the sexual. There were tendencies to hostility to passive husbands and masculine competition as well as masochistic serving. Anything which interfered with either trend produced exacerbation of the arthritis. The authors' formulation is that increase of dammed up aggression produces muscle spasms which may precipitate an arthritic attack. The authors recognize that these psychodynamic factors are present in many patients who do not develop arthritis and that there must be some factors in addition to increased muscle tone which contribute to the etiology of the arthritis.

62

Transactions of the First Conference, March 3–4, 1947, New York. Edited by Milton J. E. Senn. (Publication of Josiah Macy, Jr. Foundation. Pp. 70. Price 75 cents.)

M. Evans

M. Evans

M. Evans  

This is a report of informal discussions on the earliest relationships of parents to offspring, in which psychiatrists, pediatricians, obstetricians, anthropologists and social workers took part.

There was general agreement on the great importance of the earliest mother-child relationship for the source of later illness, and on its being a common experience for both mother and baby to be unhappy under conditions prevailing in most hospitals. It was considered desirable that the nursing couple should be continuously together, with nursing by the mother encouraged and much less emphasis on rigid schedules, etc.

The importance of pre-natal pediatric interviews was stressed, in view of the fact that the pregnant woman is often more apprehensive about her role as mother than her ability to deliver a viable infant. It was claimed that the incidence of breast feeding could be increased as a result of these interviews.

Hope was expressed that the movement towards the better understanding of the earliest mother-child relationship would attract more humanistic young doctors into obstetrics, since it was found that obstetricians are usually psychologically reactionary and less interested in people than other specialists.

68

Frank Berner

Frank Berner

Psychoanalytic Review, 1948, Vol. XXXV, No. 3, pp. 290–294.

The following views are presented: 1. That paranoia and schizophrenia, in the interest of differential diagnosis, ought always to be sharply distinguished from each other. 2. That the Freudian concept of a repressed homosexual component in paranoia has a limited rather than general applicability. 3. That the fundamental cause may very well lie in the person's effort to exclude from consciousness some painful pieces of reality. 4. That paranoia occurs among certain people when the precipitating cause is strong or intense enough to activate the latent predisposition; and 5. That paranoia represents, primarily, a disturbance of the individual's emotional or instinctual life.

'On Hallowe'en.'

Richard Sterba. The American Imago, November, 1948, Vol. 5, No. 3, pp. 213–224.

Sidney Tarachow

Sidney Tarachow

Sidney Tarachow  

The name Hallowe'en is an abbreviation of All Hallows' Evening, the evening before All Hallows' Day; it is the evening before All Saints' Day. On this day, in pagan times, the dead were supposed to have freedom to return to the homesteads which they had inhabited while alive, and all superstitions, customs and magical practices of the occasion served the purpose of warding off the dead souls, or appeasing them so that the living could pass through the dangerous night and day unharmed. In Austria the custom has remained in the shape of almost universal, compulsive devotion to the dead carried out by ritualistic visiting of the graves. No fear is thus felt. In the United States the conscious connection with the dead is denied. The fear of the dead is displaced on to children, who plague their elders, demand tribute, and frighten us by masquerading as witches ghosts, fierce creatures or as the dead. The idenafication of our children with the dead works in two directions. In addition to fearing them as symbols of the dead, we also kill them and offer them as a sacrifice to the dead. This element of sacrifice is completely repressed.

250

By Wendell Muncie. (St. Louis, Missouri: C. V. Mosby, 1948. Second edition. Pp. 620.)

Phyllis Greenacre

Phyllis Greenacre

Phyllis Greenacre  

258

This text-book, intended for medical students and written by a devoted student of Dr. Adolf Meyer, is dedicated to a presentation of Dr. Meyer's point of view and teaching; and appreciated as such in a preface written by Dr. Meyer for the first edition and now reprinted in the second. It is therefore of interest to the host of psychiatrists who came directly or indirectly under Dr. Meyer's influence as teacher, clinician and guide during the more than twenty-five years that he was Professor of Psychiatry in the Johns Hopkins Medical School—the period which was to see psychiatry emerge from the hospital background to become an active extramural therapy and a force in the community.

The book is carefully organized, conveniently arranged, and well printed. Its contents are divided into three main sections. The first part (120 pp.) deals with 'Psychobiology, the study of Normal Behaviour' which is presented through outlines of the personality study, generally autobiographical, which was required of all students of psychiatry at Hopkins. The outline, here, deals so much, however, with various tests, that one misses somewhat the sense of vision of a developing personality which is so enticingly promised (p. 29) in the stated aim 'to acquaint the student with the sense of importance of the meanings in activity, unfolding as personal biography, a purpose not unlike that of the historian and dramatist.' The second part (378 pp.), the bulk of the book, deals with 'Abnormal Behaviour, Pathology and Psychiatry', and the third part (94 pp.) is devoted to treatment, the last three pages constituting a short chapter on mental hygiene. It is especially surprising that this last section should be so brief, since one of Meyer's great contributions to American psychiatry was his sustained support of a point of view which crystallized in the mental hygiene movement.

In the treatment of distressed people Meyer had unusual gifts of enduring patience and an extraordinary capacity for rapport, sometimes more visual than verbal, but also dependent on tone of voice as much as on the meaning of his words. There was a warm and slightly remote paternalism which offered hope and even inspiration to many. He did not take account of this himself, emphasizing rather a far reaching conscientious search for facts of the patients' life as a medium of therapy; yet any appreciation of Meyer's work must include this. One wishes that this could somehow have been included in the present text. Instead the case histories do not really come to life; there is an amassing of 'facts' and little indication of their utilization in therapy. For a text which suggests a biographical approach there is singularly little awareness of the determining experiences of early childhood.

Our Inner Conflicts: A Constructive Theory of Neurosis

By Karen Horney, M.D. (London: Kegan Paul. Pp. 250. Price 10 s. 6 d.)

Marjorie Brierley

Marjorie Brierley

Marjorie Brierley  

The publisher's description of this book contains the following summary of its contents 'In Our Inner Conflicts the author has developed a theory of neurosis whose dynamic centre is a basic conflict between the attitudes of "moving toward", "moving against" and "moving away from" people.' A Preface and Introduction give a brief account of the growth of the author's views out of her personal experience in clinical work and of their crystallization in earlier lectures and books. Part I, 'Neurotic Conflicts and Attempts at Solution', includes eight chapters; Part II, 'Consequences of Unresolved Conflicts', four chapters; there is a Conclusion, 'Resolution of Neurotic Conflicts', and an Index.

The three attitudes 'compliance', 'aggression' and 'detachment' are valid behaviouristic generalizations, though over-simplified, which have a certain descriptive and practical value in personology, inasmuch as the prevailing attitude may be an index of the balance of drives and the distribution of positive and negative relationships in the person concerned.

66

Inside the Asylum

By John Vincent. (London: Allen and Unwin. Pp. 114. Price 6 s.)

H. Ezriel

H. Ezriel

H. Ezriel  

In this little book, fluently written and easily read, the author gives an account of the development of his personality, his psychological problems and his difficulties in social and sexual relations. As far as one can judge from the case history given in this book, the writer essentially suffers from a character neurosis which should have been treated outside a mental hospital. The psycho-analyst will find nothing new or particularly interesting in this autobiographical history.

The value of this book lies in a different sphere. It gives an extremely truthful, and by no means exaggerated, description of how this patient tried in vain to secure treatment for his psychological difficulties. He describes doctors who said there was nothing wrong with him or gave him a 'bottle'; others who had no time to listen to him; an unqualified 'psychologist' who exploited his sufferings until he could pay no more; the atmosphere of a mental hospital, into which his despair finally drove him.

All this is a stirring challenge to the conscience of a society that does so little to provide treatment and facilities for research and prevention in the field of psychological medicine.

65

The Objective Method of Dream Interpretation.

By Satya-Nand, M.B., B.S., I.M.S. (rtd.), P.C.M.S. (Lahore: Northern India Printing and Publishing Co. Pp. iv + 251. No price given.)

Herbert Rosenfeld

Herbert Rosenfeld

Herbert Rosenfeld  

The author claims that he has found the objective method of dream analysis. In the Introduction he criticizes all present methods of dream analysis and interpretation and sums them up by stating that 'to a scientific mind the divergent views of the dream analysts and the methods followed by them sound no better than mumbo-jumbo'. His comments on psycho-analysis are typical for the author's approach. 'I think the Freudian interpretations do not add much to the specific feature of self-knowledge of that particular person.' Later on he says, 'If Adler had two diagnoses, that of inferiority and superiority, Freud did not have more than four, the active and the passive, and the positive and the negative Oedipus complex, and all human beings must fall under these headings.' The method of dream analysis which the author has evolved does not use interpretation in the usual sense. In order to get associations to a dream, the author uses hypnosis, associations and 'reminiscence' or 'the samadhi state', which latter is his most important method of approach. In this the analysand is asked to concentrate actively and to remember facts related to words and situations depicted in the dream. Over and over again, the patient has to try and concentrate on each of the elements of one particular dream. Thousands of facts are so collected, and one might wonder what the end result of this enormous accumulation of material could be.

The main content of the book is two complete dream analyses. The first dream reads as follows: 'My sister and another vague figure were climbing a ladder along with me. The ladder was winding up. The third person fell off. I woke up in a state of dread and fear. I was rushing down to help him. I could not find the body.' After all the associations and memories are collected the analyst makes a summary of the associations and asks the analysand to make one also. The analyst does not communicate his summary to the analysand, but asks him to study the various dream elements and to criticize them until both patient and analyst are satisfied with the result. The gist of the analyst's summary of all the collected facts reads like this: 'I faced the problem of selecting a subject for my thesis (present conflict)—I am afraid my emotionalism (represented by my brother-in-law and others) may come in my way. I have almost decided to study the difficult subject of comparative psycho-analysis. This is a long and arduous ladder to climb. The brother-in-law, etc.—my individualist propensities—personified make fun of my slow pace. (Which was linked up with the history of his father.) He may be neglecting his son (his thesis) and the thesis might have fallen. The third person represents also his lower self. The anxiety is an over-fulfilment of a wish to suppress the lower self. The higher self may fall off also. There are numerous references to members of the family which are generally interpreted as standing for the patient's self.

The Question of Lay Analysis

By Sigmund Freud. Translated by N. Procter-Gregg. (Imago Publishing Co. Pp. 81. Price 9 s.)

Joan Riviere

Joan Riviere

Joan Riviere  

Those who know this short book by Professor Freud will be much pleased to have it at last in an English rendering. Apart from its intrinsic interest, it provides us with another introduction to the study of psycho-analysis, of which we cannot have too many, and this time again from the hand of the originator himself—in a form perhaps more easy and acceptable than any other, because more limited in scope.

The book appeared in the original in 1926, and it is unfortunate that for a variety of reasons it was not possible for it to be produced here before now. As a very short, exquisitely lucid and simple exposition of the nature of psycho-analytic therapy, its value is so great in relation to the need for such books—and to the acute difficulty felt by less masterly hands in writing them—that one can hardly escape a regret about its title. The Question of Lay Analysis is a challenge to the medical profession which they may not care to attend to, while the general public, as Freud himself remarks, are entirely indifferent about the matter. Provided you cure them, they do not mind who you are. Its title therefore may perhaps detract considerably from the interest it should arouse. It is important that all those in medicine and psychology who recognize the value of psycho-analysis should appreciate that this short book consists in fact of a description of what the therapy is, and does not devote more than a few pages at the end to the question of whether the analyst must be medically qualified or not.

By means of this outline of psycho-analytic technique which constitutes the main part of the book, Professor Freud ultimately makes clear that his own advocacy for the existence of duly trained and registered non-medical analysts is firmly founded on the realities of the problem, uninfluenced by prepossessions or irrelevant considerations. The theory of psycho-analysis is no easy matter to assimilate, the technique far from easy to acquire. Certain natural talents are indispensable; certain character-traits essential—a high degree of integrity, patience, self-control and capacity for self-abnegation. Wide experience of life and technical dexterity are required. The practice of analytic therapy should be permitted to those persons and only to those who have these qualifications. Medical degrees can neither be said to cover nor to replace these requirements.

responseSet.title: Analysis of a Cartoon in a Case of Hypochondriasis
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Analysis of a Cartoon in a Case of Hypochondriasis

Lois Munro

Lois Munro

Lois Munro
LONDON

Lois Munro  

The cartoon, published in the Daily Mail, September 22, 1947, is a comment on the position of the mandatory power in Palestine. It depicts a gourd-shaped container out of whose neck projects the head of a British solider and within whose body are a Jew and an Arab facing each other with teeth bared, one holding a revolver and the other a knife. The soldier's face is congested and anxious both from the constriction around his chest and the pressure from the inner contents, which pressure is indicated by the steam escaping from the neck.

The cartoon was handed to me by a man of forty-two years, three weeks after he had started analysis, in October, 1947, with the words 'This is a picture of me. I am the soldier and this is what is going on inside me.' I was so struck by the appositeness of this communication in respect of his symptoms and object relations, and the reference it contained both to his unconscious phantasies and to an incident in his early life, that I considered it a suitable means of presenting some of this patient's problems.

53
