responseSet.title: 'Individual Differences of Behaviour in Early Infancy, and an Objective Method of Recording Them: Michael Balint.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

'Individual Differences of Behaviour in Early Infancy, and an Objective Method of Recording Them

Michael Balint.

W. Hoffer

W. Hoffer

W. Hoffer  

Journ. Genetic Psychology, 1948, Vol. LXXIII, pp. 57–79 and 81–117.

Searching for individual differences of behaviour in infancy the author has found that the sucking behaviour of bottle-fed infants 'is considerably more complex, but at the same time more constant in its details, than described hitherto'. The most important result of this carefully planned experiment is the constancy of rhythms of sucking. 'Each infant seems to possess his individual rhythm (or rhythms) and keeps up its frequency even under widely varying conditions.'

Four main rhythms have been found, the most interesting among them one called 'quivering'. It is a fast rhythm of smaller amplitudes than that of sucking. 'It may occur when the infant is pausing or it may be superimposed on the sucking movements proper.' Quivering has been found to be more frequent in girls than in boys.

It has not been possible to show how the different individual rhythms have come to be adopted, but it is noteworthy that such differences exist from the very beginning of extrauterine life onwards and that these differences seem to be maintained throughout the sucking period. The author asks whether they do not form a basis of character traits.

In this paper Dr. Balint has refrained from carrying his investigation into the field of psycho-analytic theory, but he discusses 'the psycho-analytic approach' in his introductory paragraphs. He has certainly succeeded in arousing the psycho-analyst's interest and curiosity in the nature of the sucking rhythms, in the biopsychological conditions of their individual variations and their constancy.

177

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

'On Some Psychodynamics of Masochism.'

Bernhard Berliner. Psychoanalytic Quarterly, 1947, Vol. XVI, No. 4, p. 459.

Sylvan Keiser M.D.

Sylvan Keiser

Sylvan Keiser  

Masochism is a character neurosis and should be considered a defence mechanism of the ego against an instinctual conflict. There is identification with the hating or punishing love object. The aggressiveness of the masochist is not the manifestation of his primary sadism. He imitates in his search for love, to be the way he believes he is supposed to be. He incorporates the sadism of the love object through the oral need for the object's love and turns that sadism against himself. The subject's libido is blended with the sadism of the other person to completely disguise the original aggressor.

61

responseSet.title: What is Psychoanalysis: By Ernest Jones.  (International Universities Press, New York, 1948.)
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

What is Psychoanalysis

By Ernest Jones. (International Universities Press, New York, 1948.)

William Needles

William Needles

William Needles  

The main body of this text remains essentially unaltered since its original publication in England, in 1928, and in the United States a year later. A final chapter has been added in which Jones delineates the most significant developments in psycho-analysis in the intervening period. With material that fills entire libraries to dispose of Jones has had to omit much of importance but he accomplishes the task of judicious selection and achieves succintness of presentation without sacrifice of clarity.

It is hardly feasible to summarize here the already highly condensed account of the basic findings and postulates of psycho-analysis that is offered in this book but it is reassuring, in this current era of analytic controversy and apostasy, to learn from this account how little modification the basic formulations have required with the passage of time, how the foundation stands firm though the superstructure may vary in this detail or that.

Regarding the developments of the last twenty years, Jones regards the following as especially significant: the contributions of Melanie Klein, with her specialized technique for child analysis, her concept of very early Oedipus complex and super-ego formation, and her stress on infantile aggressiveness; the culturalist approach to personality study, typified by Karen Horney; the application of analytic methods to psychosomatic problems and to the psychoses; the challenge to Freud's unflattering psychology of women; the detailed study of the ego and its mechanisms of defence made by Anna Freud; the formulation of a death instinct; and the further application of psycho-analysis for the elucidation of problems in the sphere of politics and sociology.

It is somewhat surprising to note that the terms, 'repression' and 'suppression' are used interchangeably in this book, without distinction as to whether the motivation is conscious or unconscious in a given case.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Hamlet

By William Shakespeare. With a Psycho-Analytic Study, by Ernest Jones, M.D. (London: Vision. Pp. 180. Price 8 s. 6 d.)

M. L.

M. L.

M. L.  

In 1923 Ernest Jones's 'Psycho-Analytic Study of Hamlet' (based on earlier essays, 1910 and 1911) was published as Chapter I of his Essays in Applied Psycho-Analysis, which was reviewed in this Journal. To-day, a shortened form of it appears together with the text of the play.

He first speaks of the play as a creation, in relation to its author, showing the penetration and comprehensiveness of Shakespeare's understanding of obsessional neurosis, its symptoms, its symbolism, and the deeper depression underlying it. He illustrates the origin of Hamlet's (hitherto unexplained) hesitation and indecision in the ambivalence of an unresolved and repressed Oedipus complex. He stresses the importance of the Oedipal ideas in relation to both parents, the main emphasis (as other writers have recognized) being on the incestuous ideas, without which the murder of the king would have been of less importance, but more simply and directly avenged.

Finally, he compares the plot of the play with those of the earlier plays and sagas from which Shakespeare derived it. In this way he shows how Shakespeare's interest was in the subtle drawing of a character of this particular type, rather than the more obvious 'man of action' feigning madness for political reasons, not psychological ones, portrayed in them.

The longer essay, besides giving more detail and multiplying references and quotations, develops the theme of the psychic splitting and duplicating of objects in Hamlet's relations to Laertes, Horatio, Ophelia, and Polonius. In the shortened version much of this is left out.

The text of the play (described only on the dustcover) is 'a carefully edited combination of the Quarto and Folio texts, aiming at presenting the closest approximation to what Shakespeare wrote'.

64

responseSet.title: 'Alcoholism and Addiction.': Ernest Simmel.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

'Alcoholism and Addiction.'

Ernest Simmel.

Sylvan Keiser

Sylvan Keiser

Sylvan Keiser  

Psychoanalytic Quarterly, Vol. XVII, 1948, No. 1, p. 6.

The author's first premise is that the effect of alcohol depends on the use that the ego derives from it. He then differentiates four groups: the social drinker, the reactive drinker, the neurotic drinker and the alcoholic addict. In the first two groups alcohol defends the ego against external circumstances, whereas it serves the latter two classes to defend their ego against unconscious conflicts. The social drinker is defined as one who is dependent on the consumption of liquor for the enjoyment of social companionship. The reactive drinker suffers too much deprivation or suppression of instinctual desires in his personal life.

The neurotic drinker seeks to escape from his self-inflicted misery. Unconscious incestuous identifications may approach the surface and precipitate alcoholism in the neurotic. The elation revives and replaces orgastic sensations experienced in the remote past. It releases instinctual energies withdrawn from the object. This pattern of behaviour very often repeats the way he hid his masturbation from his parents. These patients frequently have had parents whose attitudes combined an incestuous seductiveness along with a puritanical attitude toward sex. Thus multiple super-egos are developed.

If the ego regresses beyond the phallic, the anal and oral stage (gastro-intestinal stage), the alcoholic becomes an addict.

The elation re-establishes the infantile pleasure principle within the conscious mind of the alcoholic. The urge for sexual contact with an object is replaced by a desire to devour it—the original identification by devouring. Many of his patients were caught as children in the midst of an orgastic elation. In bouts of drinking the addict becomes again a baby with only one object—his mother.

Everything connected with acting out their addiction has symbolic meaning—drink the last drop—to finish baby's bottle, beer and urethral pleasures, etc.

All of his alcoholic patients had deep-seated hatred for the mother. This he satisfied by seeking to devour her, drink her, but that endangered his security with her.

responseSet.title: Authoritarianism and Intolerance
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Authoritarianism and Intolerance

James Moloney M.D.

James Moloney

James Clark Moloney, M.D.
THE HAVEN SANITARIUM

James Moloney  

Intolerance of a radical and exaggerated degree occurs in the ordinary behaviour of some children. With annihilating vigour, these children furiously attack the differences, social, educational, physical, religious, economical and racial, exhibited by other children. To them, differences prevent the 'oiling of the troubled waters' with calming identifications. In many cultures such children similarly capable of intense intolerances 'gang together' their degradations upon the frightening dissimilarities. This process is scarcely questioned; it is accepted as normal instinctual manifestation. When similar children grow up into an adult community, intolerance is repressed, socialized, or is converted into racial prejudice—a prejudice that periodically sneaks out from under the lid. Racial prejudice subsumes war.

Racial prejudice is a symptom of the disease that stems from authoritarianism. Authoritarianism evolves from the misuse of authority. In this cilization the 'significant parent' almost always abuses her authority. As a result of this abuse, children develop into either neurotic conformists or neurotic nonconformists, the respective format depending upon the consistency and character of the parental pressures.1 Ackerman and Jahoda2 describe a virulent type of anti-semitism expressed by some psychopaths and paranold characters. These specific psychopathies belong to the group of neurotic nonconformists. The exaggerated nonconformist is either criminalistic or psychotic, or both. To be sure, his mental illness is usually of sufficient magnitude to warrant institutionalization. Neurotic nonconformists respond to discipline of any kind, to authoritarianism, and to the manifestations of expected authority with a character conditioned reaction. This character conditioning first occurred in infancy or early childhood at the moment the child vanquished a parent who was exerting pressure against him. This 'vanquishing of a parent' reaction becomes the format, a reflexogenous format, set off by any encounter with authority.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Psychology of Women: Vol. I, Girlhood; Vol. II, Motherhood

By Helene Deutsch, M.D. with a Foreword by Stanley Cobb, M.D. (London: Research Books Ltd., 1946 and 1947. Pp. xi + 312 and vi + 439. Price 21 s. and 25 s.)

Marjorie Brierley

Marjorie Brierley

Marjorie Brierley  

In a Foreword to Vol. I, Dr. Stanley Cobb justly calls this book 'a real contribution, a great storehouse of information about feminine psychology'. This description is even more appropriate to Vols. I and II taken together as two parts of one whole.

The major aim of the work is to explain the psychology of normal women and to indicate the modes of conflict-solution and personality development that may be regarded as typical of feminine mental health. The author uses her own vast clinical experience together with case histories supplied by other physicians and social workers, and routine hospital records, to throw the 'normal' into relief by contrast with its exaggerations in various pathological states. She attaches due importance to the interplay of biological and sociological factors in the development of feminine character but emphasizes throughout the irreducibly psychological determinants, 'the individual emotional experiences and the conflicts connected with them'. Infancy and childhood are not given special chapters but are considered in relation to the phases and problems of adolescence and adulthood. Each volume is complete in itself but the two are naturally complementary. The formal division corresponds to what the author regards as the fundamental duality of women, i.e., as person and as servant of the species.

The first volume deals with three themes: (1) the development of the distinctive 'feminine core' of personality during the transition from childhood to adulthood, (2) the analysis of this 'feminine core' and (3) the analysis of non-feminine or 'masculine' aspects of femininity. The first theme occupies Chapters I–IV, which deal with 'Pre-Puberty', 'Early Puberty', 'Puberty and Adolescence' and 'Menstruation'. Pre-puberty designates the terminal stage of the latency period (approx. ten to twelve years) which is homosexual rather than heterosexual and is characterized by a 'thrust of activity' and a 'turn toward reality'. This is the phase in which the struggle for psychological emancipation from the parents, particularly from the mother, begins and it is reminiscent of the earlier period (one and a half to three years) during which relative physical independence is acquired. The struggle for independence continues throughout the remaining pubertal phases. Early puberty is bi-sexual in character and repeats infantile 'wavering' between father and mother objects. Only adolescence is normally characterized by strong heterosexual tendencies and repeats the earlier Oedipal situation. By the close of adolescence the narcissistic-passive-masochistic 'feminine core' of personality is normally stabilized. The components of this nucleus are considered in detail in Chapters V–VII and the 'active' woman, homosexuality and the influence of the environment in Chapters VIII–X.

responseSet.title: A Technical Problem in the Treatment of the Infant Patient
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

A Technical Problem in the Treatment of the Infant Patient

Betty Joseph

Betty Joseph

Betty Joseph  

Very young children do not come to us for treatment, they are brought. More correctly, a mother and child come to a clinic because of some difficulty. The fact that neither know at all fully what is the matter will not surprise the psycho-analyst, the fact that the pædiatrician, or his co-workers, treats the matter as one of maladjustment between two people, mother and child, seldom surprises the mother; the puzzle begins when the situation is viewed theoretically. Put briefly the question is 'When does a person begin to be "self-contained"?' or viewed more administratively 'when is "the patient" attending a clinic one person and not (however small the unit) a "social aggregate"?' Such questions are raised by the following cases.

Anna at eight months could not be taken on 'buses or trams because she would start to cry and get into such a panic that her parents could not stand it. This had been going on since the age of five months. Here there was a young, gentle, happy mother with a comfortable home. For some reason I asked this mother how she travelled; she suddenly went very pink and said that she had never been able to go on 'buses up to the time when she left school, she used to feel so bad that she actually vomited. I did not attempt to analyse or try in any way to understand what lay behind the mother's problem. All we did was to discuss it openly and see how it must have happened that when Anna first started to cry on the 'bus, it seemed to have 'touched off' her own forgotten, early panic so that she was thrown out of gear and was unable to comfort the child. After we had linked the two things she began to see just how she had handled Anna on the 'bus. She would, for example, attempt to divert her attention, talk to her, make her think of something else and so on—for all the world behaving as if there really were something to be afraid of (as in her own unconscious, of course, there was). Some time later the mother told me that, after our last talk, she had taken Anna on the 'bus, how she had started to cry, but this time the mother felt all right, reassured the child without any diverting, etc., and in a moment the crying had stopped. The return ride was perfectly peaceful. This case was followed up after about fifteen months. There has been no recurrence of the problem, and Anna now very much enjoys 'bus trips.

responseSet.title: Psychiatry in Nursing: By Raymond Headlee, M.D. and Bonnie Wills Corey, R.N.  (New York:  Rinehart.  Pp. 308.)
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Psychiatry in Nursing

By Raymond Headlee, M.D. and Bonnie Wills Corey, R.N. (New York: Rinehart. Pp. 308.)

P. Goolker

P. Goolker

P. Goolker  

Teaching of Psychiatry to nurses presents many difficulties. The limited time allotted to the course challenges the instructors' ingenuity in the selection of material and in the adequate presentation of the subjects selected. The interest of the nurses in the general subject varies a great deal. While some nurses consider Psychiatry just another course others sense its broader significance and are dissatisfied with superficial outlines.

The authors have set for themselves a high goal. They aimed 'to treat the nurse as a mature, responsible person, —to inculcate in the nurse 'an intelligent and informed attitude toward mental illness and its role in the production of and the complication of organic diaease'. They have well succeeded in their efforts. The sections dealing with general psychiatric problems stress and develop the dynamic approach. Psycho-analytic thinking and orientation are very well represented. The general high tone of the book bids one to be lenient with such loose remarks as 'gross lesions of entire body as compulsion neurosis or psychosis'.

The authors have introduced some changes in classification which may be confusing to the young students in their collateral reading or in discussion of cases.

63

responseSet.title: Man for Himself, an Inquiry Into the Psychology of Ethics: By Erich Fromm.  (New York:  Rinehart.  Pp. 254.)
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Man for Himself, an Inquiry Into the Psychology of Ethics

By Erich Fromm. (New York: Rinehart. Pp. 254.)

Geza Roheim

Geza Roheim

Geza Roheim  

Reading Fromm's book leaves me with a very ambivalent feeling. The author certainly has something to say but the way he says it gives it a very different meaning from what I think it really signifies.

Humanistic ethics should be based not on taboo but on the principle of productivity. The building of the tower of Babel or Prometheus stealing fire are the symbols of human productivity (p. 149). That of course is the style that characterizes this way of thinking. A myth is taken at its face value, it is not analysed, the Unconscious has sunk into oblivion. If we analyse the myth we see that the Tower of Babel is an erection symbol, the stealing of fire myths mean stealing 'heat' from the gods and both symbolize Eros.

'Genuine love is an expression of productiveness' (p. 129) the author says, whereas of course love is derived from the sexual impulse and the sexual impulse is really 'productiveness' in a biological sense.

One dream analysis will suffice to show how the author arrives at his 'anagogic' (Silberer, Jung) constructs.

A well known writer was offered a position where he would have had to sell his integrity as a writer for a lot of money and fame while considering whether or not to accept the offer he had this dream.

At the foot of a mountain he sees two very successful men whom he despises for their opportunism, they tell him to drive up the narrow road to the peak. He follows their advice and when almost at the top of the mountain his car falls off the road and he is killed (p. 165).

Fromm says the message of this dream needs little interpretation; while he slept he knew that the acceptance of the offered position would be equivalent to destruction, not of course to his physical death …, but to his destruction as an integrated, productive human being.'

All that is needed nowadays in 'psycho-analysis' (?) to write a new book is to forget about the unconscious.

The position offered and the mountain peak (narrow passage!) both mean coitus with mother (driving!) and the fall is the work of the super-ego.

responseSet.title: 'The Question of Lay Psycho-Analysis.': Glover, Edward.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

'The Question of Lay Psycho-Analysis.'

Glover, Edward.

D. Hardcastle

D. Hardcastle

D. Hardcastle  

Brit. med. Jour., 1948, Vol. II, p. 308.

Glover reviews Freud's essay on lay analysis (Sigmund Freud: The Question of Lay Analysis. Translated by Nancy Proctor Gregg. London: Imago Publishing Co., 1947, Pp. 81. 9 s.) in the light of the nationalization of teaching hospitals and clinics. He is of the opinion that psycho-analysis will have to be more closely affiliated with teaching organizations, which, in turn, will involve more official scrutiny of training methods and selection of candidates. He endorses Freud's statement that a layman who has been analysed and trained in the technique of analysis and knows how to handle transferences and resistances is no longer a layman in psycho-analysis, but he is of the opinion that diagnosis and recommendation for treatment should remain a medical responsibility.

In considering the present position of the lay analyst in relation to psychiatrists he points out that in many clinics psychiatrists have accepted the 'play therapist' in the treatment of children almost regardless of their training. For practical purposes he postulates that a solution might be reached whereby mental disorders were classified into two groups: one suitable for lay and the other for medical analysts, the former to include anxiety hysterias, a few conversion hysterias, most obsessional neuroses, general character difficulties (excluding psychotic types) sexual inhibitions and perversions and most anti-social disorders; the latter to include the psychoses, border line psychotic conditions and psychotic character cases, severe cases of psychopathy, most conversion hysterias, some of the anxiety states, traumatic neuroses, psycho-somatic disorders, alcoholism and drug addiction.

In discussing the background of the psycho-analyst he emphasizes the importance of cultural development, wide knowledge and understanding of such subjects as anthropology, social development and sexual disorders. He advances the view that the discipline involved in a medical or Ph.D. training blunts the fine ear necessary in analysis.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

'A Case of Hypnotically Produced Secondary and Tertiary Personalities.'

Harry C. Leavitt.

Frank Berner

Frank Berner

Frank Berner  

Psychoanalytic Review, 1947, Vol. XXXIV, No. 3, pp. 274–295.

A case report is presented of a twenty-year-old man suffering from right hysterical hemiplegia whereby the experimental application of hypnosis produced secondary and tertiary personalities. There were marked contrasts in the personalities with regard to ideation, philosophies, mannerisms and attitudes, and in various instinctual manifestations. One personality presented a tendency towards self-criticism, severe conscience, and lofty ideals. The other personality was a direct opposite. He was markedly hedonistic, narcissistic, dishonest and given to uninhibited sexuality.

responseSet.title: 'Three "Battles" During Analytic Treatment.': Edmund Bergler.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

'Three "Battles" During Analytic Treatment.'

Edmund Bergler.

Frank Berner

Frank Berner

Frank Berner  

Psychoanalytic Review, 1948, Vol. XXXV, No. 3, pp. 273–284.

In every analysis three battles are being fought unconsciously by the patient: 1. The unconscious Ego's fight with and against the torturing inner conscience, i.e. a battle with Super-Ego. 2. The unconscious Ego's fight against the progress of the neurosis per se. 3. Intensification of the battle against the neurosis through 'mobilization of unconscious guilt'. This intensification is artificially induced through the analysis. The patient relives, in projection upon the harmless and innocent analyst, his by-gone conflicts. By putting the displaced inner guilt at the place where it belongs genetically, the 'unproductively misused' guilt is mobilized in a 'productive' way—thus destroying in favourable cases the patient's neurosis.

178

responseSet.title: 'A Psycho-Analytic Concept of the Origin of Depression.': W. Clifford M. Scott,
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

'A Psycho-Analytic Concept of the Origin of Depression.'

W. Clifford M. Scott,

D. Hardcastle

D. Hardcastle

D. Hardcastle  

Brit. Med. J., 1948, Vol. I, P. 538.

Scott regards the earliest form of depression as being that feeling which emerges out of the realization first that it is the same self that can both love and hate, secondly that the ego can love and hate the same object or part object and thirdly that the same object can be both gratifying or frustrating, or can appear to be loving and hating. This situation occurs when that degree of integration has been reached which allows the infant to believe in the continuity of the self or part self and objects or part objects. The time at which this situation occurs will depend on the degree of maturity at birth, the constitutional intellectual development and the quality of the previous emotional development. Tolerance for it will depend again on past experiences particularly the relationship between love and hate and the belief that love is stronger than hate and can annul the feelings of damage which result from hate, while intolerance is related to the belief in the omnipotence of aggressive acts, impulses and imaginations directed towards that object which when not frustrating is gratifying.

In briefly outlining the development of the infant to that stage which allows depression to appear, scott shows how pleasurable feelings associated with the infants' normal activities (their breathing, suckling, evacuation, etc.) or the angry feelings arising from interference with the free working of these activities are identified not only with the activity itself but also with the organs involved. Furthermore they overflow on to any other activity which may be occurring simultaneously.

178

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Sexual Behaviour in the Human Male

By Alfred C. Kinsey, W. B. Pomeroy and S. E. Martin. (W. B. Saunders Co., Philadelphia and London, 1948. Pp. 804.)

Paul Goolker

Paul Goolker

Paul Goolker  

This book, usually referred to as the 'Kinsey Report', is a first progress report in a long series to be published in the next twenty years and has aroused enormous public interest. The popular appeal of the book is increased by the fact that it is based on interviews with people in all walks of life. In the avalanche of reviews in the lay press, and scientific journals—some critics hailed it as a milestone in science and others were quick to exploit it as a basis to revise legislation covering divorce and sexual crimes. Rigid enforcement of the present laws, according to Kinsey, could put 95 per cent. of the people in jail. On the other hand, some reviewers attacked it as a dangerous assault on family life and morals, for they feared sensational and distorted interpretations of the facts.

Kinsey's aim was 'an attempt to accumulate an objectively determined body of fact about sex which strictly avoids social or moral interpretations of the fact'. He wished to learn 'what people do sexually, what factors account for differences in sexual behaviour among individuals and among various segments of the population'. The broad scope of the investigation involving thousands of individuals—the first attempt on such a large scale—make an exhaustive review unfeasive. In a sense, this review takes on the character of a review of reviews because the writer is influenced by the large number of criticisms he has read.

The Kinsey project is based on the taxonomic approach, which studies groups of individuals which stand as representatives of a species to discover identities and differences between and among the various groups. In the taxonomic approach great care must be observed in choosing and delimiting the sample studied and in the Kinsey study of the sex habits of the human male it was determined that about 300 cases were desirable for a sample in a group homogeneous for sex, age, educational level and other significant factors.

The senior author based his technique on a long experience in insect taxonomy which he now combined with the interviewing techniques used in public opinion sampling. He was, however, aware of the difficulties where 'human personalities had to be considered and human memories utilized'. The book is based on personal interviews with 5, 300 white males, mostly from the north-eastern quarter of the United States. The authors state: 'The continental European patterns of sex behaviour are so distinct from the American (as our own European sampling already indicated) that no additions of the European to the American data should ever be made.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

New Possibilities in Private Psychiatric Practice

C. P. Oberndorf. American Journal of Psychiatry, Vol. 105, No. 8, February, 1949, pp. 589–593.

Martin Grotjahn M.D.

Martin Grotjahn

Martin Grotjahn  

The isolating nature of psychiatric work, especially if it is psycho-analytic work, tends to limit the range of the physician's vision and philosophy and may nourish any tendency he may have towards autocracy. He may become prone to choose as his patients those cases with which experience has taught him his technique is likely to be most successful. This further restricts the range of activity and may make the psycho-analyst narrow, opinionated and intolerant of techniques other than his own. Oberndorf repeats the advice that the analyst continue his own analysis in intervals of five years, but the self-satisfaction produced by successful practice is usually so great that few men are willing to resubmit themselves to the critical scrutiny of a new psycho-analysis. To counteract this pernicious tendency the analyst should associate himself with a psychiatric clinic or general hospital.

It is also desirable for private psychiatrists to join in a group practice. In such groups it would be desirable to have psychiatrists of varied interests, such as psycho-analysts, psycho-analytically inclined psychotherapists, therapists rarely using psychoanalytical techniques but rather the re-educational, suggestive, and persuasive approaches, or milder forms of ambulatory shock treatment. The group should also contain psychiatrists specializing in certain forms of mental illness, such as alcoholism, or in the care of disturbed children and adolescents, and those serving the parents of average children in an advisory capacity.—The practice of psychiatry is likely to expand further provided it can supply better service to a larger number of people.

Oberndorf's paper amounts to a thoughtful statement 'on psychiatry for psychaitrists and psychoanalysts'. Perhaps the author could have put some more emphasis on the healthy influence which teaching has on the psychiatric teacher. To a certain extent, psychiatric and psycho-analytic teaching safeguards the teacher against the dangers of growing old and possibly rigid.

'The Equivalents of Matricide.'

Robert M. Lindner

Sylvan Keiser

Sylvan Keiser

Sylvan Keiser  

Psychoanalytic Quarterly, Vol. XVII, No. 4, 1948.

A variety of clinical syndromes are presented in which matricidal drives are manifested. The cases studied range from the murder of the mother substitute in the form of a young girl, to suicide, to its representation in symptoms and dreams. The need for further study of this problem is stressed since the entire emotional gamut from passive-receptive to aggressive-hostile feelings are initially experienced between mother and child.

[[RunningHead]]

'Treatment of a Case of Peptic Ulcer and Personality Disorder.'

Franz Alexander. Psychosomatic Medicine, 1947, Vol. IX, No. 5, pp. 320–330.

Sidney Tarachow

Sidney Tarachow

Sidney Tarachow  

The author treated a twenty-three-year-old male student who presented a continuous five-year history of peptic ulcer confirmed by X-ray. The treatment consisted of thirty-six analytic sessions during a period of ten months. Patient and doctor sat face to face. The author presented the material to illustrate both the method of brief therapy as well as the psychosomatic correlations. The first step was to allow the patient to gratify his dependent needs in the treatment without arousing shame. Defensive reactions to this craving thus became unnecessary: the patient was able to overcome denial of dependence, incapacity to show emotion and various defensive aggressions and guilt reactions.

62

responseSet.title: Children in Need: By Melitta Schmideberg.  (Allen and Unwin, London.  Price 12 s. 6 d.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Children in Need

By Melitta Schmideberg. (Allen and Unwin, London. Price 12 s. 6 d.

Augusta Bonnard

Augusta Bonnard

Augusta Bonnard  

This book is not written for psycho-analysts, nevertheless they would profit from reading it, not only for the mass of terse clinical, sociological and legalistic material that it contains, but also from the sense of pride that they would gain. Psycho-analysts are often accused of being a remote set of people, akin to bacteriologists, though perhaps less practical, who only study and pronounce upon distorted and minute aspects of Life which, meanwhile, surges painfully unaltered and unassisted, outside their quiet windows. Dr. Schmideberg's book does much to correct this view. It shows how psycho-analytic knowledge can be used as a swift and accurate instrument for the precise exploration and relief of human misery; not for the few, but for the many, and not merely in the timeless shelter of the consulting room, but in the rush of urgent events. Her main thesis and aim in writing this book is, however, to show how, in our comparatively human society, it is still current practice, whether through official ignorance or inertia, to force such intolerable strains on the underprivileged child, that it is turned from being an underdog into a pariah dog. Her evidence makes it clear that, if children are deprived of the means of making satisfactory object relationships, they may be expected to grow up into delinquents, severe neurotics or psychopaths. While she also shows that she is well aware that such personalities can be produced in unsatisfactory private homes, she is at pains to prove that it is precisely when Authority steps in and removes such children, in order to send them into present-day institutions, that final and irreparable damage is done to them. Dr. Schmideberg's supporting official evidence is provided by quotations from the Curtis Report, which reveals many residential institutions as loveless places, run by ill-chosen staff, mainly concerned with the enforcement of disciplines.

The remedy that Dr. Schmideberg suggests for the improvement of the nation's mental health is that of a vast extension of psychiatric social services.

responseSet.title: Ernst Simmel and Freudian Philosophy
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Ernst Simmel and Freudian Philosophy11

Max Horkheimer

Max Horkheimer

Max Horkheimer  

It may sound unorthodox to speak about Simmel and Freud as philosophers. The very concept of a Freudian philosophy appears almost as a contradiction in terms. Is not psychology a science and science clearly separated from philosophy? The word philosophy seems to remind us of rationalizations of unconscious wishes, hypostatization of dreams and ideologies, the very objects unveiled by Freud's analytical research. In disregard of existing taboos, he dared look behind the cloak of lofty ideas and ideals and made it his task to trace back individual as well as social habits and attitudes to primitive biological drives. The conflict of these drives with the prevailing framework of civilization served him as the principle of explanation in order to debunk not a few of the religious and philosophical entities which people like to offer as their motives. Freud and his most congenial disciples, among whom Ernst Simmel certainly belonged, were the relentless enemies of intellectual super-structures including the metaphysical hiding places of the mind. It was his credo 'that there is no other source of knowledge of the Universe but the intellectual processing of carefully verified observation, in fact what is called Research, and that no knowledge can be obtained from revelation, intuition, or inspiration'. Does not Simmel's clear voice ring in our ears when we read how Freud quoted Heinrich Heine's persiflage of the idealistic philosopher: 'With his nightcap and his nightshirt-tappers, he botches up the loopholes in the structure of the world.'

Yet, Freud's negative attitude against philosophical illusions is itself an expression of his unique approach to the decisive problems of life, an approach of the kind we may well call philosophy. Only we must understand the term philosophy in an unconventional way, free from connotations of an idealization of the gruesome realities of our world. In contrast to such dubious teleological efforts of humanity there have always been other meanings of philosophy. Here philosophy signifies the theoretical belief that without truth there is no human life, and the will to make uncompromising truth the leading principle of one's life, the subordination to this goal of all personal interests. Furthermore, philosophy means the ability to have new and genuine experiences, the power to overcome the hypnotic spell of current ideologies, to resist the deadening effect of daily routines on our organs of perception, and to open new horizons in our understanding of nature and humanity. Both elements, the passion for truth and the capacity to raise the human mind on new levels (of understanding rather than of domination) are parts of the definition of philosophy, and real philosophers have always understood it in that sense.

responseSet.title: On the Psycho-Analytic Training System
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

On the Psycho-Analytic Training System11

Michael Balint

Michael Balint

Michael Balint
LONDON

Michael Balint  

I

It has become a commonplace to say that a new world was discovered by Freud: the world of the Unconscious. To mankind this discovery was a traumatic experience and all sorts of defensive mechanisms were mobilized against it. Gradually the defensive mechanisms gave way to a genuine interest, and recently mankind started to turn to us—as Freud is no longer among us—for information and guidance. This means that in fact we are coming to be regarded as guides by mankind.

Perhaps the most relevant, though not the only function of our guiding and teaching activity, is the training of future analysts. How we discharge this duty, what results we achieve in this field, will deeply influence not only the future of our profession and of our science, but also the destiny of mankind.

Considering this heavy responsibility it is surprising to discover that there is practically no literature on psycho-analytical training. In all the volumes of the Zeitschrift, Imago, Journal and Quarterly there are only two articles published on this subject. One is Dr. Sachs' posthumous paper (1947), a charming causerie of a wise old man, giving some sound advice and carefully avoiding any tricky problem. I shall come back presently to the second paper, as well as to Freud's paper: 'Analysis Terminable and Interminable', where a short chapter, in fact the shortest, consisting only of three pages, discusses the training.

Probing further into the matter, I found it reported that at the Innsbruck Congress in 1927, three prominent training analysts each read a paper to the International Training Committee, i.e. only to the members of the Training Committees of the Branch Societies, not even to all 'training analysts'. These were: Rado: 'Aufbau des Psychoanalytischen Lehrganges', Sachs: 'Lehranalyse', H. Deutsch: 'Kontrollanalyse'. The summaries were not printed, but the report promised that all three papers would be published in full. In fact none of them has been printed.

Then a long pause followed. The next record is from 1935, the first Four Countries' Conference in Vienna. The first theme for discussion was: 'Die didaktische Analyse und die Analysenkontrolle.' This meeting was open to all full members of the International Association present. The two openers were H. Deutsch and I. Hermann. Although there was a lively interest and one of the most fruitful discussions in my experience developed, we are faced with the same fate: no summaries were given and none of the papers was published.

responseSet.title: Some Embryological Neurological, Psychiatric and Psycho-Analytic Implications of the Body Scheme
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Some Embryological Neurological, Psychiatric and Psycho-Analytic Implications of the Body Scheme11

W. Clifford Scott

W. Clifford Scott

W. Clifford M. Scott
LONDON

W. Clifford Scott  

This paper is an attempt to do little more than survey several sciences that deal with facts which have to be integrated to build up a concept of a real ego—or body scheme (B.S.)—as it will be referred to in this paper. The material given, especially in the psycho-analytic section, together with my own numerous comments, is so brief that it will need to be elaborated in further papers.

The problem of the nature of the 'reality ego' is being brought forward chiefly for three reasons. First, it is related to attempts at clarifying the conception of early development and the implications of early development on later life. Anything that may add to the clarity of this recurrent topic should be of some little value. Secondly, this problem furnishes, I think, a fruitful field for co-operative research amongst embryologists, neurologists, psychiatrists and psycho-analysts. Such co-operation can deal with observations made on the same persons or can deal with the best method of formulating and describing the facts observed by different observers in different persons. The aim should be to use as few new terms as possible, and the use of different terms for the same facts observed by different types of observers should be avoided. Some proposed topics of research may lead to widening the acceptance of psycho-analytic theory—others may lead to new knowledge. It is to this latter view that the problem of the B.S. may be related. The third, and most important reason of all for discussing these problems is my increasing conviction that a new orientation as to what has been formulated as the relation between the id and the ego is necessary. There is no doubt about the gradual enlargement of psycho-analytic views of the importance of what is unconscious and of what can become conscious. There is doubt regarding the difficulties we have perhaps created for ourselves by trying to clarify a relationship between the psyche and the soma, between the body and the mind, between the psychic ego and the bodily ego, almost as if this relationship were real in the same sense that the relationship between one ego and any other ego is real. I have come gradually to the conclusion that more progress may be made if psycho-analysts attempt to view this division between the psychic ego and the body ego as symptomatic—as a symptom of splitting which may not necessarily be egosyntonic. So far during our life-time the conceptions, which determine our view of the world and what happens in it, have altered markedly. We are part of that world and we believe that there is a relationship between the unconscious forces in the discoverer and the discoveries which are made of forces in the world.

responseSet.title: A Contribution to the Theory of Anxiety and Guilt
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

A Contribution to the Theory of Anxiety and Guilt

Melanie Klein

Melanie Klein

Melanie Klein  

In this paper I propose to present my conclusions regarding anxiety and guilt. These conclusions have been gradually evolved over a number of years, and it may be useful to retrace some of the steps by which I arrived at them.

I

Concerning the origins of anxiety, Freud to begin with put forward the hypothesis that anxiety arises out of a direct transformation of libido. In Inhibitions, Symptoms and Anxiety1 Freud reviewed his various theories on the origin of anxiety. As he put it: 'I propose to assemble, quite impartially, all the facts that we do know about anxiety and to give up the idea of making any immediate synthesis of them.'2 He stated again that anxiety arises from direct transformation of libido but now seemed to attribute less importance to this 'economic' aspect of the origin of anxiety. He qualified this view in the following statements: 'The whole matter can be clarified, I think, if we commit ourselves to the definite statement that as a result of repression the intended course of the excitatory process in the id does not occur at all; the ego succeeds in inhibiting or deflecting it. If this is so the problem of "transformation of affect" under repression disappears.'3 And: 'The problem of how anxiety arises in connection with repression may be no simple one; but we may legitimately maintain the opinion that the ego is the actual seat of anxiety and give up our earlier view that the cathectic energy of a repressed impulse is automatically turned into anxiety."4

Regarding the manifestations of anxiety in young children, Freud said that anxiety is caused by the child 'missing someone who is loved and longed for'.5 In connection with the girl's most fundamental anxiety, he described the infantile fear of loss of love in terms which in some measure seem to apply to infants of both sexes: 'If the mother is absent or has withdrawn her love from the child, it can no longer be certain that its needs will be satisfied and may be exposed to the most painful feelings of tension'.6

responseSet.title: The Nature and Function of Phantasy
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Nature and Function of Phantasy11

Susan Isaacs

Susan Isaacs

Susan Isaacs
LONDON

Susan Isaacs  

INTRODUCTION

A survey of contributions to psycho-analytic theory would show that the term 'phantasy' has been used in varying senses at different times and by different authors. Its current usages have widened considerably from its earliest meanings.

Much of this widening of the concept has so far been left implicit. The time is ripe to consider the meaning and definition of the term more explicitly.

When the meaning of a technical term does become extended in this way, whether deliberately or insensibly, it is usually for a good reason—because the facts and the theoretical formulations they necessitate require it.2 It is the relationships between the facts which need to be looked at more closely and clarified in our thoughts. This paper is mostly concerned with the definition of 'phantasy'; that is to say, with describing the series of facts which the use of the term helps us to identify, to organize and to relate to other significant series of facts.

1A chapter from a book in preparation jointly with Paula Heimann, Melanie Klein and Joan Riviere.2In a contribution to the British Psycho-Analytic Society in 1943, Dr. Ernest Jones commented with regard to this extension of the meaning of 'phantasy': 'I am reminded of a similar situation years ago with the word "sexuality". The critics complained that Freud was changing the meaning of this word, and Freud himself once or twice seemed to assent to this way of putting it, but I always protested that he made no change in the meaning of the word itself: what he did was to extend the conception and, by giving it a fuller content, to make it more comprehensive. This process would seem to be inevitable in psycho-analytical work, since many conceptions, e.g. that of conscience, which were previously known only in their conscious sense, must be widened when we add to this their unconscious significance.'73

responseSet.title: Inferences from the Dream Screen
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Inferences from the Dream Screen

Bertram Lewin M.D.

Bertram Lewin

Bertram D. Lewin, M.D.
NEW YORK

Bertram Lewin  

In a previous communication (1946), a special structure, the dream screen, was distinguished from the rest of the dream and defined as the blank background upon which the dream picture appears to be projected. The term was suggested by the motion pictures; because, like its analogue in the cinema, the dream screen is either not noted by the dreaming spectator, or it is ignored due to the interest in the pictures and action that appear on it. However, under certain circumstances, the screen plays a rôle of its own and becomes perceptible. Then it enters to alter what is called the form of the dream.

As we know from Freud's discussion (1933a), peculiarities in the manifest form are apt to have their origin in the latent content. The dream screen is no exception to this rule. It has a definite meaning in itself, and in view of its assumed ubiquity it is not surprising that the meaning should be general. The dream screen represents the idea of 'sleep'; it is the element of the dream that betokens the fulfilment of the cardinal wish to sleep, which Freud considered responsible for all dreaming. Also, it represents the maternal breast, usually flattened out, as the infant might perceive it while falling asleep. It appears to be the equivalent or the continuation, in sleep, of the breast hallucinated in certain predormescent states, occasionally observed in adults (Isakower, 1938).

In M. J. Eisler's view (1922), each falling asleep psychologically repeats the events that take place in the baby when it falls asleep after nursing. Accordingly, the blank dream screen would approximate the baby's state of mind in sleep. The prototypic dream would be visually blank; it would consist only of the dream screen, that is to say, of the flattened surface of the recently suckled breast (or its equivalent), plus whatever elementary disturbing sensations might enter from other external or internal fields of perception. Dreams with precisely this content do occur, even in adults, and on analysis are found to be complete fulfilments of the wish to sleep at the maternal breast after nursing. Examples were given in my paper, and their occurrence has been confirmed by Nunberg and Atkin.1 Ferenczi (1934), I have since found, has reported visually blank dreams with 'lower segment' sensations; because the dreams were repeated, he interpreted them, to support his later theory of the neuroses, as the result of early trauma. In my view, the element of repetition represents the latent thought of repeated feeding experiences, a topic to be dealt with below.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

'Projection, Extrajection and Objectivation.'

Eduardo Weiss. Psychoanalytic Quarterly, Vol. XVI, No. 3, p. 357.

Sylvan Keiser M.D.

Sylvan Keiser

Sylvan Keiser  

The term projection is discussed and new subdivisions are described. Extrajection is designated as the converse of intrajection which is the transformation of a part of the ego into an object representation and which had been included under the general term of projection. The object representation which emerges will be called the extraject. True projection is a falsification of the external world, e.g. an hallucination. Objectivation is when a subject finds actual traits of an extraject in a real object.

When an 'intraject' is extrajected it shall be called re-extrajection. These terms are discussed in detail and material is offered to demonstrate their clinical usefulness.

61

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Symposium on the Evaluation of Therapeutic Results11

Clarence Oberndorf , Phillis Greenacre and Lawrence Kubie

Lawrence Kubie

Lawrence Kubie  

Dr. EDWARD BIBRING:

This symposium is due to the initiative of Dr. Oberndorf, who for many years has been interested in investigating the results of psycho-analytic treatment in particular and psychotherapy in general. Some time ago he suggested that a more systematic investigation of this kind be undertaken by the Boston Institute. We felt, however, that we should start from the grass roots and discuss the methods and principles of such a research project first. This evening's discussion is only a beginning to—as I hope—a successful end.

Dr. Oberndorf will state the problem, Dr. Greenacre will discuss the problems of method and Dr. Kubie the principles and organization of such research.

Dr. CLARENCE P. OBERNDORF:

I wish to congratulate the Boston Psycho-analytic Institute as being the first group to hold a symposium to investigate psycho-analytic therapy. It is particularly appropriate that it should be held in Boston for, as you may know, the first analytic treatment of any patient in the world was held in the neighbourhood of Boston in Colonial times when the little known analyst, Dr. Roger Chillingworth undertook the treatment of the Reverend Dimmesdale who was suffering from fatigue, exhaustion, loss of weight, listlessness. Dr. Chillingworth in approaching this patient found that the psychosomatic complaints, in our current terminology, were due to Dimmesdale's association with one Hester Prynne, an attractive lady who wore the scarlet 'A'. Dr. Chillingworth said: 'We must know the man before we begin to do him good.' If you go back to Nathaniel Hawthorne's Scarlet Letter, you will find that Dr. Chillingworth had employed the best—most modern—psycho-analytic approach in many instances with his patient.

Also, Dr. James Jackson, Professor of Medicine at Harvard, and I think Visiting Physician at the Massachusetts General Hospital, in 1885 in writing of the treatment of dyspepsia said: 'I have had to decant upon the evils and the impropriety, if not the sin of over-conscientiousness.' And quoting from memory, 'it has often been necessary for me to give my patient a lecture about sensuosity, sometimes even when the patient wore a black coat'.

responseSet.title: On Neurotic Disorders of Sensibility and Body Scheme—A Bioanalytic Approach to Pain, Fear and Repression
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

On Neurotic Disorders of Sensibility and Body Scheme—A Bioanalytic Approach to Pain, Fear and Repression11

Joachim Flescher M.D.

Joachim Flescher

Joachim Flescher, M.D.
ROME

Joachim Flescher  

I intend to study here the nature and origin of a mechanism which in certain morbid conditions obscures the psychological distinction between our physical Ego and the outside world. As we shall see later, this occurrence is intimately linked with the neurotic disorders of sensibility. Among the senses, those which bring us into intimate relationship with the stimulating object and thus play a greater rôle in our instinctual life, merit special interest. Of those senses the most important one is that which conveys the biologically most significant sensation, pain, which besides being communicated by specific receptors, results also from excessive stimulation of any sensitive organ.

We know hyperalgesia and analgesia in hysteria as neurotic symptoms. The organicists have failed to explain these symptoms by localizing elements, and only the psycho-analytic approach has succeeded so far in connecting them with the mechanism described as conversion, i.e. translation of psychic conflicts into physical disorders. Moreover, the so-called hysterical analgesia is not due to a simple interruption of the sensations just before they are conveyed to the cortex; for painful stimuli of the analgesic zone are not only not perceived but often even fail to produce the relative vasomotor and pupillar reactions. Only by a wider interpretation of the dominion and range of psychical force and even more of the instinctual impulses which form their energy basis, is it possible to understand why sensory suggestion, formerly considered a process altering only the act of perception, in reality loses its effect through the interruption of the sympathetic nerve ways. Thus hysterical anæsthesia, which some authors define as 'negative hallucinations', may also give rise to local changes of unquestionably organic nature. This fact constitutes an important step towards the understanding of those organic alterations which pathogenetically derive from psychic motives. I hope, however, to show that in certain phenomena the way from the psychic to the organic sphere is also used in the opposite direction. This is certainly true in the phenomenon of pain.

Sandor Lorand

Sandor Lorand  

It is with profound sorrow, fresh from the shock of his sudden death, that I try to express my feelings about the man who was a fatherly friend to me and to many others; the founder of our Society, 1 its president for many years, and honorary president until his death.

It was a long and difficult road which he travelled. Way back in 1908 he accompanied Freud to Clark University for the lectures which Freud described as the official inauguration of psycho-analysis in America. On that occasion Freud was honoured and acclaimed and one would have thought that Dr. Brill, who accepted the torch from the hand of Freud, would have been able to continue unobstructed.

We all know about those early years when Brill's courage and unfaltering faith in analysis gave him the tenacity and energy to continue on the straight path he had cut out for himself. He never wavered, never compromised. It is because of the richly productive work of A. A. Brill that analysis holds the place it does in America to-day.

1Read at the New York Psycho-Analytic Institute, March 30, 1948.2

[[RunningHead]]

Recreation and the Total Personality

By S. R. Slavson. (New York: Association Press Pp. x + 205. Price $3.)

Marjorie Brierley

Marjorie Brierley

Marjorie Brierley  

This is an American book directed in the first instance to an American public but it could be read with profit by social workers in this or any other country which aims at raising the psychological standard of life for its inhabitants. It is a very practical handbook for persons actively engaged in recreational work and the chapters on theory are necessarily general and simple but include a sound minimum. The author's wide practical experience, theoretical knowledge and native insight have combined to produce a lively appreciation of basic psychological principles and a balanced outlook. Thus, although he is an expert on Group Therapy (a Lecturer in the School of Education of New York University, he is also Director of Group Therapy for the Jewish Board of Guardians and President of the American Group Therapy Association) his attitude is emphatically personalist; the stress throughout this book is on the need for flexible adaptation of recreactional outlets to individual needs.

The author deplores the too commonly accepted antithesis between education and recreation and urges that they have in common the furtherance of personal integration and 'total health'. Recreation can be integrative and prophylactic to the extent that it provides satisfying uses for impulses otherwise unemployable and thus mitigates the burden of 'civilized' frustration. For instance, under present-day conditions spontaneity tends to be unduly discouraged in both child and adult and should therefore be preserved at all costs in recreation. Recreation is not superfluous indulgence or dispensable 'amusement' but a necessary corrective of many unwholesome trends in modern life; it should be regarded as a vital factor in the promotion of personal and social well-being.

66

[[RunningHead]]

The Metamorphosis of Philosophy

By John Oulton Wisdom. (Al-Maaref Press, Cairo. British agent, Basil Blackwell, Oxford. Pp. vii + 224. Price 12 s. 6 d.)

Marjorie Brierley

Marjorie Brierley

Marjorie Brierley  

The author of this book is already known to Journal readers as a pioneer in the application of psycho-analysis to the study of speculative philosophy, e.g. through his illustrative papers 'The Unconscious Origin of Schopenhauer's Philosophy' (this Journal, 1945, XXVI, 44) and the more recent 'Three Dreams of Descartes' (1947, XXVIII, 11). Studies of this kind are less convincing than direct analyses of living persons but, whatever their drawbacks, these papers amply support the author's general thesis, i.e. 'The statements of a speculative philosopher do not directly express facts about the universe but symptomatically express facts about himself—they form his unconscious autobiography' (177).

This conception is called 'The Psychocentric Hypothesis' and forms the title and subject-matter of the third section of the book. 'Psychocentrics is thus the use of psycho-analysis in the anthropological study of philosophy' (218). The coinage of the new term may not appear strictly necessary to psycho-analysts but the word 'psychocentric' (apart from its etymological suitability, discussed 182–84) undoubtedly emphasises the personal motivation of speculative systems. The philosopher tends to be a psychological solipsist, interested in other people only in so far as they play a part in his world. Speculation is a flight from reality to mentation; a philosophical system creates a world nearer to the heart's desire than the world of reality. However, the author shows clearly that these private worlds are more than simple wishfulfilments; they serve both defensive and sublimatory purposes and the expression they afford to the otherwise inexpressible is not only stabilizing to their creators, but indicative of their unconscious preoccupations. Hence, although the author advocates the transformation of speculative thought into Philosophical Psychocentrics (the metamorphosis of philosophy), he is well aware that it is useless to attempt to argue a philosopher out of his opinions.

[[RunningHead]]

Studies of the 'Free' Art Expression of Behaviour Problem Children and Adolescents as a Means of Diagnosis and Therapy

By Margaret Naumburg. (New York: Nervous and Mental Disease Monographs, 1947. Pp. xi + 225. Price $5.50.)

W. Clifford Scott

W. Clifford Scott

W. Clifford Scott  

The work done at the New York State Psychiatric Institute and Hospital in art periods once or twice a week as one aspect of a research project with behaviour problem children is recounted in detail. The six chapters which comprise this work had previously been published in four separate Journals. The problems presented by the children were: a 9-year-old compulsively destructive boy, a 9-year-old untruthful, truanting boy, a 11-year-old boy with tics, a 5-year-old aggressive boy, a 10-year-old boy with severe intellectual inhibition, and a 15-year-old, anxious, amnesic, hysterical girl. Ninety-nine drawings were reproduced.

The patients were all severely ill and were under active psychotherapy at the same time. Only occasionally is a connection between what happened in the art periods and the psychotherapeutic interviews with the psychiatrists given. The author stresses the value of 'spontaneity' and the 'creative process' in personality development and criticizes Freud (New Introductory Lectures on Psycho-analysis) for 'emphasizing the protective value to the individual of the mechanism of repetition and the necessity of accepting education as of use in order to "inhibit", forbid and suppress'. Throughout the book there is considerable emphasis on spontaneity, creation, sublimations, and the emergence of archetypal forms, but this does not prevent a serious attempt to use the material provided to understand other unconscious processes.

This book will not add to the knowledge of child psycho-analysts, but nevertheless should be read by them as it is a book which will without doubt stimulate, inform and encourage many psychotherapists of children to more intensive work.

69

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Concept of Psychogenesis

By John R. Reid. Amer. J. Psychiat., 1948, 104, No. 10. Pp. 653–60.

J. Wisdom

J. Wisdom

J. Wisdom  

A plea is made to save time by agreeing on definitions of important words, in particular on a semantical analysis of 'psycho-genesis'. This depends on definitions of 'mental event' and 'interpretation'. Any stimulus-object can acquire meaning. Thus a red light in certain situations means 'stop!' The meaning is an 'interpretation' of the stimulus. It is held that 'interpretation' in this sense is characteristic of mental events. And an event is mental if (and only if?) it is an interpretative reaction to a stimulus-object.

Turning to 'psychogenesis', 'we follow the common sense empirical method of asking ourselves under what circumstances do we say that a person's disorder is psychogenic'. 'Any syndrome, disorder, or disease is "psychogenic" if (and only if?) among the various etiological factors … there is at least one … called "an interpretation"'.

It is implied that a patient has at some time been aware of certain events if he has reacted to them interpretatively. The author follows Bernfeld's assumption that all unconscious thoughts must have once been conscious, so that the approach of this paper covers 'unconscious mental events'. There may be difficulties about this, 'subliminal cues', and the question whether interpretations must be 'emotionally reinforced'.

Tests are given for deciding whether an event B is psychogenic: there must be some prior interpretation A such that (i) B occurs, if and only if A occurred and (ii) if A varies, then B varies.

The following is therefore arrived at: a disorder is psychogenic if some interpretation has preceded it without which we have reason to believe the disorder would not have occurred.

138

'Ego Functioning in Two Types of Dreams.'

Milton L. Miller.

Sylvan Keiser

Sylvan Keiser

Sylvan Keiser  

Psychoanalytic Quarterly, Vol. XVII, 1948, No. 3, p. 346.

The analysis of mirror fantasies or dreams reveals that they represent basic conflicts emerging from analysis and that they are clues to the narcissistic defences against them. The dreams cited in the paper demonstrate the patient's attempt to face the unconscious conflict, to look at it and to bring it into focus with reality.

As an example of the opposite reaction, in analysis dreams of catastrophe are cited. Such dreams reveal that more affect has been mobilized by the analysis than can be mastered by the integrative mechanism of the ego. By exaggerating the danger the patient rationalizes his retreat from his fear. The variations in the nature of the catastrophe afford valuable clues to the gradual changes in the integrative capacity of the ego.

179

[[RunningHead]]

Understandable Psychiatry

By Leland E. Hinsie, M.D. (The Macmillan Company, New York, 1948. Pp. 359. Price $4.50.)

Sandor Lorand

Sandor Lorand

Sandor Lorand  

Many of the numerous popular and semi-popular books written about facts and theories of dynamic psychiatry have confused rather than enlightened the reader. Dr. Hinsie's book stands out as a happy exception. His vast experience in psychiatry combined with his facility in writing result in a competent and systematic presentation, and his convincing style will enable the lay reader to understand the meaning of neuroses and psychoses. He bases the book on the premise that psychiatry is the one branch of medicine most understandable to the public and on the fact that psychiatric treatment cannot be accomplished by the doctor alone, but calls for closest co-operation between patient and physician. Therefore Dr. Hinsie proceeds to help the lay reader to know more about himself so that he may be better equipped to realize the differences between healthy adjustment and disease or maladjustment.

The first nine chapters of the book abound in illustrations which clearly demonstrate to the reader how emotions may cause illness. Dr. Hinsie explains how Freud's contributions make human behaviour more easily understood, and throughout the book emphasizes the complex factors in the individual's environment which foster the development of his specific neurotic reaction.

In the second part of his book he gives a clear picture of the different types of psychoses, schizophrenia, epilepsy, alcoholism, and psychopathic personalities. For those who need an authentic account of the theories and facts with which psychiatry deals this book will be a real guide.

259

responseSet.title: The Death of Hamlet's Father
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Death of Hamlet's Father

Ernest Jones

Ernest Jones

Ernest Jones  

When a poet takes an old theme from which to create a work of art it is always interesting, and often instructive, to note the respects in which he changes elements in the story. Much of what we glean of Shakespeare's personality is derived from such studies, the direct biographical details being so sparse. The difference in the accounts given in Hamlet of the way the King had died from that given in the original story is so striking that it would seem worth while to look closer at the matter.

The most obvious difference is that in the Saxo-Belleforest saga the murder is a public one, with Shakespeare a secret one. We do not know, however, who made this change, since an English play called Hamlet, thought to be written by Kyd, was extant some twelve years before Shakespeare wrote his; and he doubtless used it as well as the Belleforest version. That play no longer exists except in a much later and much distorted German version, but a Ghost probably appeared in it, and one can hardly imagine any other function for him than to disclose a secret murder. There is reason to suppose that Shakespeare may himself have had a hand in the Kyd play, but at all events he made the best possible use of the alteration.

In the old saga Claudius (there called Feng) draws his sword on his brother the King (Horvendil)1 at a banquet and slays him 'with many wounds'. He explains to the assembled nobles that he has done this to protect his sister-in-law (Geruth) from ill-treatment and imminent peril of her life at the hands of her husband—a pretext evidently a reflection of the infant's sadistic conception of coitus. Incidentally, in the Saxo saga (though not with Belleforest), there had here been no previous adultery with the Queen, so that Feng is the sole villain, and Amleth, unlike Hamlet, unhesitatingly kills him and reigns in his stead as soon as he can overcome the external obstacles. In Hamlet, as is well known, the plot is intensified by the previous incestuous adultery of the Queen, which convulses Hamlet at least as much as his father's murder and results in an animus against women that complicates his previously simple task.

responseSet.title: On Szondi's 'Schicksalsanalyse' and 'Triebdiagnostik'
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

On Szondi's 'Schicksalsanalyse' and 'Triebdiagnostik'

Michael Balint

Michael Balint

Michael Balint
LONDON

Michael Balint  

L. Szondi Schicksalsanalyse. Wahl in Liebe, Freundschaft, Beruf, Krankheit und Tod. (Fate Analysis. Choice in Love, Friendship, Profession, Illness and Death.) Second edition. Basel: Benno Schwabe und Co., 1948. Pp. 422. Price Sw. Fr. 28.50.

Experimentelle Triebdiagnostik. Band I. Test-Band, Band II. Text-Band. (Experimental Diagnosis of Instincts.) Bern: Hans Huber, 1947. Pp. xvi + 308. Price Sw. Fr. 68.

I

The starting point of Szondi's investigations was the observation that man is attracted by persons who show similar symptoms to one or the other of his ancestors. As explanation of this very frequent occurrence he puts forward two hypotheses, (a) that man inherits the genes of the illness from his ancestors, but that they are in a recessive state in him, kept in biological 'repression' by their normal, dominant allels, (b) that although these receissive 'repressed' genes cannot influence his phenotype (he remains manifestly healthy) they do determine his choice of love objects; he is bound to fall in love with someone exhibiting the phenotype the genes of which he carries in himself in a recessive state.

A psycho-analyst, of course, has a less biological, more psychological explanation. The 'ancestor' is usually an important person of the man's infantile world and in case his (the man's) libido becomes fixated on this 'ancestor, ' the man's choice of love objects is thereby severely restricted, he will be attracted only by people on whom a transference of his emotions is possible because they are in some way similar to his primary love object. This sequence of events can be described somewhat differently by changing our point of view. The 'ancestor', the important person of the infantile situation, is likely to be introjected, to be incorporated as an 'internal object'; in mature life then only those people will be attractive whose qualities enable our man to project one or the other of his internal objects on to them. The similarity between this latter description and Szondi's is obvious. The difference is that while our description starts with the individual history, i.e. with the extrauterine life, Szondi goes beyond that and starts his investigations with the history of the family before the birth of the individual. While neither of these descriptions gives a complete, self-contained explanation, it is not possible to refute either of them. Such a state of affairs suggests strongly that we are dealing with another case of over-determination, i.e. both descriptions are likely to be equally valid—for this particular phenomenon.

responseSet.title: Adrian Leslie Stephen 1883–1948
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Adrian Leslie Stephen 1883–1948

Anonymous

Anonymous

Anonymous  

When Adrian Stephen unfolded himself and rose, gaunt and immensely tall, to speak in our Scientific Meetings, we were always impressed by his force and his kindliness. He addressed us in slow, measured words, as if we were a Bench of very learned Judges on whose ears softly spoken words and carefully thought argument were the only possible mode of address. … At the Crematorium on May 5 of this year there was a background of unfunereal music, no word was said, no name mentioned. At a predetermined moment, none knew when or by whom the signal was given, and without alteration in the music, the coffin passed through those folding doors. In silence we gathered: in silence we watched his passing. Since this was his wish, reference to his life seems almost a breach of faith with the dead.

He was, however, a representative of a culture that has almost passed away. That generation of stern and brilliant thinkers among whom he lived did much to shape the course of England's destiny—their books we daily use, their formulations we still employ, and their lonely son we now mourn.

Adrian Leslie Stephen was born on October 27, 1883, the second son of Sir Leslie Stephen by his second marriage. His father was one of the Editors of the Dictionary of National Biography, his uncle, Sir James Fitzjames Stephen (1829–94), was the Jurist, author of General View of the Criminal Law of England (1863, 1890), and his aunt, Caroline Emilia Stephen (1834–1909), was famous among quietist religious communities for her piety and an almost strange mysticism. Going back a generation earlier, his grandfather was Sir James Stephen (1789–1859), Colonial Under-Secretary, author of Essays in Ecclesiastical Biography, whose brother, Sir George (1794–1879), lawyer and author, emigrated to Australia, where he died, and whose cousin, Sir Alfred (1802–1894), was Chief Justice of New South Wales. All these men were knighted for distinguished public service in law or administration, or to letters. The family's circle included Fredk. William Maitland, Lord Haldane, Watts, Henry James, George Meredith, Burne Jones, H. A. L. Fisher, Lord Morley, Lord Bryce and Lady Ritchie.

responseSet.title: Clinical Manifestations of Aggression and their Role in Psycho-Analytic Treatment
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Clinical Manifestations of Aggression and their Role in Psycho-Analytic Treatment11

S. Nacht

S. Nacht

S. Nacht
PARIS

S. Nacht  

Awareness posits opposition.—Fichte.

I

INTRODUCTION

Love thy neighbour as thyself …

Although for centuries now men have been told to love one another, they usually live in a state of mutual hatred, discord and destruction, enthusiastically following 'anti-' rather than 'pro-' movements, whatever their aim. That this should be so despite society's attempts to subdue aggression is not astonishing when one considers that life itself exacts a hostile reaction right at the very start. The first contact with his surroundings is felt by the newborn child as pain, and it takes a very long time for the resulting cry of anger to be replaced by non-aggressive responses.

Men have always known that love and hate are the very roots of their existence, but it is to Freud that we owe our really scientific investigations of these two emotions.

Apparently for many, the weight of emphasis in psycho-analytic work has been on the first emotion in its widest sense. Many psycho-analysts have found and still find that inhibited sexuality is the prime source of conflicts having a neurotic outcome.

However, it has seemed to me that those whom we are called upon to observe find greater difficulty in mastering the conflicts set up by aggressive tendencies than those of other origins. The condition necessary for lack of sexual satisfaction to have pathological effects seems to me to lie in the degree of aggression. Let us take the instance of the Oedipus complex: the traumatic element is made up not so much of the child's sexual attraction towards the parent of the opposite sex, as of aggression that he feels towards the parent of the same sex, of whom he is jealous. As for the adult, it is easier for him to find a satisfactory compromise between his sexual needs and the restrictions society imposes on him in this regard, than it is between the aggression he feels at the constant daily frustrations imposed by that society and the pressures it puts on him if he turns his aggression to action.
